etymology.story: 1570s, "residence;" see resident + -cy.
1794, intransitive, back-formation from rotation.

early 13c., from Old French conscience "conscience, innermost thoughts, desires, intentions; feelings" (12c.), from Latin conscientia "knowledge within oneself, sense of right, a moral sense," from conscientem (nominative consciens), present participle of conscire "be (mutually) aware," from com- "with," or "thoroughly" (see com-) + scire "to know" (see science).
1773, "Stock Exchange Latin" [Weekley], from Latin bonus "good" (adj.); see bene-.
early 14c., from Old French suget, subject (Modern French sujet), from Latin subiectus (see subject (n.))..
1811, past participle adjective from verb convolute (1690s), from Latin convolutus, past participle of convolvere (see convolution); or perhaps a back-formation from convolution.
1550s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + gulf (n.) or else from Old French engolfer.

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 整数；整体；完整的事物

etymology.story: "a whole number" (as opposed to a fraction), 1570s, from Latin integer (adj.) "intact, whole, complete," figuratively, "untainted, upright," literally "untouched," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + root of tangere "to touch," from PIE root *tag- "to touch, handle" (see tangent (adj.)).

late 14c., "creeping or crawling animal," from Old French reptile (early 14c.) and directly from Late Latin reptile, noun use of neuter of reptilis (adj.) "creping, crawling," from rept-, past participle stem of repere "to crawl, creep," from PIE root *rep- "to creep, crawl" (source also of Lithuanian replioju "to creep").

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 取/抓 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Medieval Latin receptivus, from Latin recipere (see receive).

"capable of living without oxygen," 1879 (as anaerobian; modern form first attested 1884), from French anaérobie, coined 1863 by French bacteriologist Louis Pasteur (1822-1895), from Greek an- "without" (see an- (1)) + aer "air" (see air (n.1)) + bios "life" (see bio-)..
early 15c., "income from property or possessions," from Middle French revenue, in Old French, "a return," noun use of fem.
late 15c., from Late Latin sufficientia, from Latin sufficiens "adequate" (see sufficient) + -cy.
"long, narrow, flat piece," mid-15c., "narrow piece of cloth," probably related to or from Middle Low German strippe "strap, thong," and from the same source as stripe (n.1).
1620s, shortened form of geographical (q.v.); in some cases probably from Middle French géographique or Late Latin geographicus..

meanings.definitionCn: 垄断,专卖,专利权,专利事业

morphology.mnemonic: 单一 + 多 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "exclusive control of a commodity or trade," 1530s, from Latin monopolium, from Greek monopolion "right of exclusive sale," from mono- + polein "to sell," from PIE root *pel- (5) "to sell" (source also of Sanskrit panate "barters, purchases," Lithuanian pelnas "gain," Old Church Slavonic splenu, Russian polon "prey, booty," Old Norse falr, Dutch veil, German feil "for sale, venal").

meanings.definitionCn: 分开， 岔开； 分歧， 相异； 偏离， 违背

etymology.story: 1660s, from Modern Latin divergere "go in different directions," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + vergere "to bend, turn" (see verge (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 否定 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French negociacion "business, trade," and directly from Latin negotiationem (nominative negotiatio) "business, traffic," noun of action from past participle stem of negotiari "carry on business, do business, act as a banker," from negotium "a business, employment, occupation, affair (public or private)," also "difficulty, pains, trouble, labor," literally "lack of leisure," from neg- "not" (see deny) + otium "ease, leisure." The sense expansion from "doing business" to also include "bargaining" about anything took place in Latin..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + celer + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin acceleratus, past participle of accelerare "to hasten,  quicken," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + celerare "hasten," from celer "swift" (see celerity).

meanings.definitionCn: 信用，信誉；[金融] 贷款；学分；信任；声望
相信，信任；把…归给，归功于；赞颂
洪水；一阵；大雨；突然迸发

etymology.story: early 15c., originally Scottish and northern English, "a sudden flood, especially one caused by heavy rains or a snowmelt," of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 加强的；集中的；透彻的；加强语气的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "intense, fervent, great," from Old French intensif (14c.) and Medieval Latin intensivus, from Latin intens-, past participle stem of intendere "turn one's attention; strain, stretch" (see intend).

meanings.definitionCn: (Alter)人名；(英)奥尔特；(德、捷、葡、爱沙、立陶、拉脱、俄、西、罗、瑞典)阿尔特

etymology.story: late 14c., "to change (something)," from Old French alterer "change, alter," from Medieval Latin alterare "to change," from Latin alter "the other (of the two)," from PIE *al- "beyond" (see alias (adv.)) + comparative suffix -ter (as in other).

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + able

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French divisibile, from Late Latin divisibilis, from divis-, past participle stem of Latin dividere (see divide (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 举例说明,阐明;图解,加插图

etymology.story: 1520s, "light up, shed light on;" 1610s, "educate by means of examples," back-formation from illustration, and in some cases from Latin illustratus, past participle of illustrare "light up, make light, illuminate." Sense of "provide pictures to explain or decorate" is 1630s.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 步/走 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1630s; see regress + -ive.
1952, acronym for self-contained underwater breathing apparatus..

meanings.definitionCn: 无法调和的， 矛盾的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 再/重新/回 + 调和/和解 + able

etymology.story: 1590s, from assimilated form of in- (1) "not, opposite of" + reconcilable, if that word is so old.

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: de + thing or animal spoken about before. + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French definitif (12c.), from Latin definitivus "explanatory, definitive," from past participle stem of definire (see define).

1550s, from Middle French nomade (16c.), from Latin Nomas (genitive Nomadis) "wandering groups in Arabia," from Greek nomas (genitive nomados, plural nomades) "roaming, roving, wandering" (to find pastures for flocks or herds), related to nomos "pasture, pasturage, grazing," literally "land allotted," from PIE root *nem- "to divide, distribute, allot" (see nemesis)..

meanings.definitionCn: 不受欢迎的， 令人不快的

etymology.story: 1660s, "not to be desired, objectionable," from un- (1) "not" + desirable.
1883, formed in English from literate + -cy.

morphology.mnemonic: 起源/开始 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1650s, probably a back-formation of origination.
late 14c., "warlike," from Latin martialis "of Mars or war," from Mars (genitive Martis), Roman god of war; see Mars.

in logic, "to 'bring in' as a conclusion of a process of reasoning," 1520s, from Latin inferre "bring into, carry in; deduce, infer, conclude, draw an inference; bring against," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + ferre "carry, bear," from PIE *bher- (1) "to bear, to carry, to take" (source also of Sanskrit bharati "carries;" Avestan baraiti "carries;" Old Persian barantiy "they carry;" Armenian berem "I carry;" Greek pherein "to carry;" Old Irish beru/berim "I catch, I bring forth;" Gothic bairan "to carry;" Old English and Old High German beran, Old Norse bera "barrow;" Old Church Slavonic birati "to take;" Russian brat' "to take," bremya "a burden").
1852 (originally in the medical sense and much scorned by the GPs); see special (adj.) + -ist.
mid-15c., from Latin transitus, past participle of transire "go or cross over" (see transient).
1785, from Latin adolescentem (nominative adolescens) "growing, near maturity, youthful," present participle of adolescere "grow up, come to maturity, ripen" (see adolescent (n.))..

late 14c., from Old French disposer (13c.) "arrange, order, control, regulate" (influenced in form by poser "to place"), from Latin disponere "put in order, arrange, distribute," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + ponere "to put, place" (past participle positus; see position (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 缩短， 削减； 剥夺

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French courtault "made short," from court "short" (Old French cort, from Latin curtus; see curt) + -ault pejorative suffix of Germanic origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 感觉/感知 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: late 14c., sentement, "personal experience, one's own feeling," from Old French sentement (12c.), from Medieval Latin sentimentum "feeling, affection, opinion," from Latin sentire "to feel" (see sense (n.)).
1660s, "bond, link, means of communication," from Latin nexus "that which ties or binds together," past participle of nectere "to bind," from PIE root *ned- "to bind, tie" (see net (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 变异，[生物] 分化；区别

morphology.mnemonic: day日 + …的人/处于…状态 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 鞘， 外壳； 套； 护皮； 紧身连衣裙

etymology.story: Old English sceað, scæð, from Proto-Germanic *skaithiz (source also of Old Saxon scethia, Old Norse skeiðir (plural), Old Frisian skethe, Middle Dutch schede, Dutch schede, Old High German skaida, German scheide "a sheath, scabbard"), according to OED, possibly from root *skei- "divide, split" (see shed (v.)) on notion of a split stick with the sword blade inserted.

meanings.definitionCn: 专利； 专利品； 专利权
批准专利， 获得专利

etymology.story: late 14c., in letters patent, literally "open letter," from Old French patente, from Latin patentum (nominative patens) "open, lying open," present participle of patere "lie open, be open," from PIE *pete- "to spread" (see pace (n.)).

1760 as a botanical term for the fertilizing element of flowers (from Linnæus, 1751), earlier "fine flour" (1520s), from Latin pollen "mill dust; fine flour," related to polenta "peeled barley," and pulvis (genitive pulveris) "dust," from PIE root *pel- (1) "dust; flour" (source also of Greek poltos "pap, porridge," Sanskrit pálalam "ground seeds," Lithuanian pelenai, Old Church Slavonic popelu, Russian pépelŭ "ashes")..

meanings.definitionCn: 典范; 范例; 示例

etymology.story: late 15c., from Late Latin paradigma "pattern, example," especially in grammar, from Greek paradeigma "pattern, model; precedent, example," from paradeiknynai "exhibit, represent," literally "show side by side," from para- "beside" (see para- (1)) + deiknynai "to show" (cognate with Latin dicere "to show;" see diction).

morphology.mnemonic: 确定 + 使…化/造成 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of certifying," from French certificat, from Medieval Latin certificatum "thing certified," noun use of neuter past participle of certificare (see certify).

meanings.definitionCn: 量子论；额；美国昆腾公司（世界领先的硬盘生产商）

etymology.story: 1610s, "one's share or portion," from Latin quantum (plural quanta) "as much as, so much as; how much? how far? how great an extent?" neuter singular of correlative pronominal adjective quantus "as much" (see quantity).

meanings.definitionCn: (of)设想,构思,想象;以为;怀胎,怀有

etymology.story: late 13c., conceiven, "take (seed) into the womb, become pregnant," from stem of Old French conceveir (Modern French concevoir), from Latin concipere (past participle conceptus) "to take in and hold; become pregnant," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + comb.

meanings.definitionCn: 辉耀的；炽热的；发白热光的

etymology.story: 1794, from French incandescent (18c.) or directly from Latin incandescentem (nominative incandescens), present participle of incandescere "become warm, glow, kindle," from in- "within" (see in- (2)) + candescere "begin to glow, become white," inceptive of candere "to glow, to shine" (see candle).

morphology.mnemonic: in + to swear profanely + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: "hostile attack," early 15c., from Old French incursion "invasion, attack, assault" (14c.) or directly from Latin incursionem (nominative incursio) "a running against, hostile attack," noun of action from past participle stem of incurrere "run into or against, rush at" (see incur)..
"to seek prey," 1974, a back-formation from predator, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 改变,变换;变压;转化;改造,改造

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 形状/形态

etymology.story: mid-14c., "change the form of" (transitive), from Old French transformer (14c.), from Latin transformare "change in shape, metamorphose," from trans- "across" (see trans-) + formare "to form" (see form (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 似非而是的话,自相矛盾的话,反论

etymology.story: 1530s, "statement contrary to common belief or expectation," from Middle French paradoxe (14c.) and directly from Latin paradoxum "paradox, statement seemingly absurd yet really true," from Greek paradoxon, noun use of neuter of adjective paradoxos "contrary to expectation, incredible," from para- "contrary to" (see para- (1)) + doxa "opinion," from dokein "to appear, seem, think" (see decent).

meanings.definitionCn: 大小；量级；[地震] 震级；重要；光度

etymology.story: 1590s, "natural, not acquired," from Latin genuinus "native, natural, innate," from root of gignere "beget" (see genus), perhaps influenced in form by contrasting adulterinus "spurious." [Alternative etymology is from Latin genu "knee," from a supposed ancient custom of a father acknowledging paternity of a newborn by placing it on his knee.] Meaning "really proceeding from its reputed source" is from 1660s.

meanings.definitionCn: 废除；撤销；废止；放弃；否定

etymology.story: 1847, applied to a species of large apes (Troglodytes gorilla) by U.S.

1748, "pamphlet; short written work stitched together," from French brochure "a stitched work," from brocher "to stitch" (sheets together), from Old French brochier "to prick, jab, pierce," from broche "pointed tool, awl" (see broach (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 免疫的,有免疫力的;有受影响的;豁免的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "free, exempt" (from taxes, tithes, sin, etc.), from Latin immunis "exempt from public service, untaxed; unburdened, not tributary," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + munis "performing services" (compare municipal), from PIE *moi-n-es-, suffixed form of root *mei- (1) "to change" (see mutable).

morphology.mnemonic: sand spout, dust storm + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1640s, noun of action from deviate (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: [化学] 化合物；混合物；复合词
合成；混合；恶化，加重；和解，妥协

etymology.story: late 14c., originally compouned, past participle of compounen (see compound (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 资助，支持；系…于桩上；把…押下打赌
强度；强烈；[电子] 亮度；紧张

etymology.story: 1660s, from intense + -ity.

meanings.definitionCn: 男性的；阳性的；男子气概的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "belonging to the male grammatical gender;" late 14c., "of men, male," from Old French masculin "of the male sex" (12c.), from Latin masculinus "male, of masculine gender," from masculus "male, masculine; worthy of a man," diminutive of mas (genitive maris) "male person, male," of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 非常的;格外的;意外的;离奇的;临时的

morphology.mnemonic: extra + 顺序/等级 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin extraordinarius "out of the common order," from extra ordinem "out of order," especially the usual order, from extra "out" (see extra-) + ordinem, accusative of ordo "order" (see order (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 涉及；使关心（concern的ing形式）；忧虑
爱慕， 吸引； 吸引力； 具有吸引力的事

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from French attraction, from Latin attractionem (nominative attractio) "a drawing together," noun of action from past participle stem of attrahere (see attract).

early 15c., "increase efficacy" (of medicine); mid-15c., "provide (a town) with walls and defenses," from Old French fortifiier (14c.) "to fortify, strengthen," from Late Latin fortificare "to strengthen, make strong," from Latin fortis "strong" (see fort) + facere "to make" (see factitious).
late 15c., "of the nature of an agreement," from Late Latin conventionalis "pertaining to convention or agreement," from Latin conventionem (see convention).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 形状/形态

etymology.story: mid-14c., confourmen, from Old French conformer "conform (to), agree (to), make or be similar, be agreeable" (13c.), from Latin conformare "to fashion, to form, to shape; educate; modify," from com- "together" (see com-) + formare "to form" (see form (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 循环， 流通； 流传， 传播； 发行， 发行量

morphology.mnemonic: ring环，圆 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French circulation or directly from Latin circulationem (nominative circulatio), noun of action from past participle stem of circulare "to form a circle," from circulus "small ring" (see circle (n.)).

mid-14c., "authoritative," from Old French autentique (13c., Modern French authentique) "authentic; canonical," and directly from Medieval Latin authenticus, from Greek authentikos "original, genuine, principal," from authentes "one acting on one's own authority," from autos "self" (see auto-) + hentes "doer, being," from PIE *sene- "to accomplish, achieve." Sense of "entitled to acceptance as factual" is first recorded mid-14c.

late 14c., from Old French evident and directly from Latin evidentem (nominative evidens) "perceptible, clear, obvious, apparent" from ex- "fully, out of" (see ex-) + videntem (nominative videns), present participle of videre "to see" (see vision)..

meanings.definitionCn: 旅馆；门房；集会处；山林小屋
[医]麻疹, 风疹, 包虫病, 痧子

etymology.story: infectious disease, early 14c., plural of Middle English masel, perhaps from Middle Dutch masel "blemish" (in plural "measles") or Middle Low German masele, from Proto-Germanic *mas- "spot, blemish" (source also of Old High German masla "blood-blister," German Masern "measles").

morphology.mnemonic: 心理/精神 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1680s; see psychology + -ical.

meanings.definitionCn: 犯罪，做错事；把交托给；指派…作战；使…承担义务

etymology.story: late 14c., "to give in charge, entrust," from Latin committere "to unite, connect, combine; to bring together," from com- "together" (see com-) + mittere "to put, send" (see mission).

morphology.mnemonic: 父亲 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-15c., "pertaining to a (Church) patriarch," from patriarch + -al, or else from Late Latin patriarchalis, from patriarcha..

morphology.mnemonic: 起源/开始 + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: 1742, from original (adj.) + -ity.

etymology.story: late 14c., envolupen, "be involved" (in sin, crime, etc.), from Old French envoleper "envelop, cover; fold up, wrap up" (10c., Modern French envelopper), from en- "in" (see en- (1)) + voloper "wrap up," which is of uncertain origin, perhaps Celtic (see Gamillscheg, Diez) or Germanic ("Century Dictionary").

etymology.story: late 15c., "a casting down" (of the evil angels from heaven), also, in alchemy "separation of a solid substance from a solution," from Middle French precipitation (15c.) and directly from Latin praecipitationem (nominative praecipitatio) "act or fact of falling headlong, haste," noun of action from past participle stem of praecipitare "fall, be hasty," from praeceps "steep" (see precipice).

meanings.definitionCn: 性格,品质,特性;人物,角色;字符,(汉)字

etymology.story: mid-14c., carecter, "symbol marked or branded on the body;" mid-15c., "symbol or drawing used in sorcery," from Old French caratere "feature, character" (13c., Modern French caractère), from Latin character, from Greek kharakter "engraved mark," also "symbol or imprint on the soul," also "instrument for marking," from kharassein "to engrave," from kharax "pointed stake," from PIE root *gher- (4) "to scrape, scratch." Meaning extended in ancient times by metaphor to "a defining quality."Meaning "sum of qualities that define a person" is from 1640s.
also on-going, 1877, from on + going (see go)..
1934, from super- + nova..

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 星 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French constellacion "constellation, conjuncture (of planets)," from Late Latin constellationem (nominative constellatio) "set with stars," from constellatus, from Latin com- "with" (see com-) + past participle of stellare "to shine," from stella "star" (see star).
mid-15c., from Latin divulgare "publish, make common," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + vulgare "make common property," from vulgus "common people" (see vulgar).
white, solid substance present in body tissues, 1894, earlier cholesterin, from French cholestrine (Chevreul, 1827), from Greek khole "bile" (see cholera) + steros "solid, stiff" (see sterility).

1540s, from Middle French crasse (16c.), from Latin crassus "solid, thick, fat; dense." The literal sense always has been rare in English; meaning "grossly stupid" is recorded from 1650s, from French.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 呼吸 + 使…/具有…的 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: early 15c., "to include," from Old French compris, past participle of comprendre "to contain, comprise" (12c.), from Latin comprehendere (see comprehend).

morphology.mnemonic: 向上/提高 + a dismissal + 与…有关的/形容词

morphology.mnemonic: br + er + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: mid-15c., "a broker's trade," from broker (n.) + -age.

"sprinkle liquid in drops," 1520s, from Middle Dutch sprayen, from Proto-Germanic *sprewjan (source also of German sprühen "to sparkle, drizzle," Spreu "chaff," literally "that which flies about"), from extended form of PIE root *sper- (4) "to sow, scatter" (see sprout (v.)).

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French distinguiss-, stem of distinguer, or directly from Latin distinguere "to separate between, keep separate, mark off, distinguish," perhaps literally "separate by pricking," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + -stinguere "to prick" (compare extinguish and Latin instinguere "to incite, impel").

morphology.mnemonic: 第一/首要 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., from Late Latin primordialis "first of all, original," from Latin primordium "a beginning, the beginning, origin, commencement," from primus "first" (see prime (adj.)) + stem of ordiri "to begin" (see order (n.)).
1560s, from Latin interspersus "strewn, scattered, sprinkled upon," past participle of *interspergere, from inter- "between" (see inter-) + spargere "to scatter" (see sparse).
early 13c., houlen, probably ultimately of imitative origin; similar formations are found in other Germanic languages.

meanings.definitionCn: 大量的；实质的；内容充实的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "ample, sizeable," from Old French substantiel (13c.) and directly from Latin substantialis "having substance or reality, material," in Late Latin "pertaining to the substance or essence," from substantia "being, essence, material" (see substance).

meanings.definitionCn: 实行;实施;执行;完成;履行;处死

also re-fuel, 1811, from re- "again" + fuel (v.).
mid-12c., from Old Danish boþ "temporary dwelling," from East Norse *boa "to dwell," from Proto-Germanic *bowan-, from PIE root *bheue- "to be, exist, grow" (see be).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + to grow long, lengthen, + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "to make long or longer," 1530s, from Late Latin elongatus, past participle of elongare "to prolong, protract" (see elongation).

etymology.story: 1540s, re-Latinized from sinthoma (late 14c.), from Medieval Latin sinthoma "symptom of a disease," altered from Late Latin symptoma, from Greek symptoma "a happening, accident, disease," from stem of sympiptein "to befall, happen; coincide, fall together," from assimilated form of syn- "together" (see syn-) + piptein "to fall," from PIE *pi-pt-, reduplicated form of root *pet- "to rush; to fly" (see petition (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 虚无主义；无政府主义；恐怖行为

etymology.story: 1817, "the doctrine of negation" (in reference to religion or morals), from German Nihilismus, from Latin nihil "nothing at all" (see nil), coined by German philosopher Friedrich Heinrich Jacobi (1743-1819).

Old English weldan (Mercian), wieldan, wealdan (West Saxon) "have power over, compel, tame, subdue" (class VII strong verb; past tense weold, past participle gewealden), merged with weak verb wyldan, both from Proto-Germanic *waldan "to rule" (source also of Old Saxon and Gothic waldan, Old Frisian walda "to govern, rule," Old Norse valda "to rule, wield, to cause," Old High German waltan, German walten "to rule, govern").

meanings.definitionCn: 独特的，稀罕的；[数] 唯一的，独一无二的

etymology.story: 1815, literal and figurative, American English, of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 机械 + ize + 状态/行为/过程

1590s, from crystal + -ize.

late 15c., from superior (adj.) + -ity, or directly from Medieval Latin superioritatem (nominative superioritas), from superior..
1620s, "pertaining to irony," from Late Latin ironicus, from Greek eironikos "dissembling, putting on a feigned ignorance," from eironeia (see irony).

late 14c., "breadth," from Old French latitude (13c.) and directly from Latin latitudo "breadth, width, extent, size," from latus "wide, broad, extensive," from Old Latin stlatus, from PIE *stleto-, suffixed form of root *stele- "to spread, to extend" (source also of Old Church Slavonic steljo "to spread out," Armenian lain "broad").

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French prosperer (14c.) and directly from Latin prosperare "cause to succeed, render happy," from prosperus "favorable, fortunate, prosperous," perhaps literally "agreeable to one's wishes," traditionally regarded as from Old Latin pro spere "according to expectation, according to one's hope," from pro "for" + ablative of spes "hope," from PIE root *spe- "to flourish, succeed, thrive, prosper" (see speed (n.))..

"enter in a hostile manner," late 15c., from Latin invadere "to go, come, or get into; enter violently, penetrate into as an enemy, assail, assault, make an attack on," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + vadere "go, walk" (see vamoose).

etymology.story: 1650s, "forecast of the probable course of a disease," from Late Latin prognosis, from Greek prognosis "foreknowledge," also, in medicine, "predicted course of a disease," from stem of progignoskein "come to know beforehand," from pro- "before" (see pro-) + gignoskein "come to know" (see gnostic).
1828, "manager or promoter of a theatrical production," reborrowing of French entrepreneur "one who undertakes or manages," agent noun from Old French entreprendre "undertake" (see enterprise).

1570s, from Middle French démocratie (14c.), from Medieval Latin democratia (13c.), from Greek demokratia "popular government," from demos "common people," originally "district" (see demotic), + kratos "rule, strength" (see -cracy)..

Old English deagian "to dye," from the source of dye (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 逆的，相反的；敌对的；不利的；有害的

etymology.story: late 14c., "contrary, opposing," from Old French avers (13c., Modern French adverse) "antagonistic, unfriendly, contrary, foreign" (as in gent avers "infidel race"), from Latin adversus "turned against, turned toward, fronting, facing," figuratively "hostile, adverse, unfavorable," past participle of advertere, from ad- "to" (see ad-) + vertere "to turn" (see versus).

1630s, "reason, rationale," from Latin ratio "reckoning, numbering, calculation; business affair, procedure," also "reason, reasoning, judgment, understanding," from rat-, past participle stem of reri "to reckon, calculate," also "think" (see reason (n.)).
late 15c., "to clip, mow;" see barb (n.).

mid-15c., "to surround by hostile stratagem," from Latin circumventus, past participle of circumvenire "to get around, be around, encircle, surround," in figurative sense "to oppress, assail, cheat," from circum "around" (see circum-) + venire "to come" (see venue).
1640s, from Latin alumnus "a pupil," literally "foster son," vestigial present passive participle of alere "to nourish" (see old), with ending akin to Greek -omenos.

meanings.definitionCn: 进入者；新会员；参加竞赛者；新工作者

etymology.story: 1630s, "one who enters, a beginner" (of professions, etc.); from French entrant, present participle of entrer (see enter).

"connoisseur in eating and drinking," 1820, from French gourmet, altered (by influence of Middle French gourmant "glutton") from Old French groume, originally "wine-taster, wine merchant's servant" (in 13c.

"of or pertaining to the side," early 15c., from Olde French latéral (14c.) and directly from Latin lateralis "belonging to the side," from latus (genitive lateris) "the side, flank; lateral surface" (see oblate (n.)).

Old English scrybb "brushwood, shrubbery," a rare and late word (but preserved also, perhaps, in Shrewsbury), possibly from a Scandinavian source (compare dialectal Danish skrub "brushwood," Norwegian skrubba "dwarf tree").

1540s, "to call by name," back-formation from nomination or else from Latin nominatus, past participle of nominare "to name, call by name, give a name to," also "name for office,"" from nomen "name" (see name (n.)).

late 14c., from Old French diametre, from Latin diametrus, from Greek diametros (gramme) "diagonal of a circle," from dia- "across, through" (see dia-) + metron "a measure" (see meter (n.2))..

mid-14c., from stem of Old French restreindre "press, push together; curb, bridle; bandage" (12c.), from Latin restringere "draw back tightly, confine, check" (see restriction).

late 14c., from Old French durabilité, from Late Latin durabilitatem (nominative durabilitas), noun of quality from Latin durabilis (see durable)..

alternative (chiefly U.S.) spelling of anaemia (q.v.).

"separate, distinct," late 14c., from Old French discret, discre, and directly from Latin discretus "separated;" see discreet.

root.meaning: seek a job by submitting an application for one

morphology.mnemonic: seek a job by submitting an application for one + ance

etymology.story: 1560s, "action of putting into use," from apply + -ance.

Old English lar "learning, what is taught, knowledge, science, doctrine, art of teaching," from Proto-Germanic *laizo (Old Saxon lera, Old Frisian lare, Middle Dutch lere, Dutch leer, Old High German lera, German Lehre "teaching, precept, doctrine"), from PIE *leis- (1) "track, furrow" (see learn)..

meanings.definitionCn: 块，团；群众，民众；大量，众多；质量
群众的，民众的；大规模的，集中的
爱好者；业余爱好者；外行

etymology.story: 1784, "one who has a taste for (something)," from French amateur "lover of," from Latin amatorem (nominative amator) "lover," agent noun from amatus, past participle of amare "to love" (see Amy).

meanings.definitionCn: [化学] 衍生物，派生物；导数

morphology.mnemonic: der + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 14c., condempner "to blame, censure," from Old French condamner "to condemn" (11c.), from Latin condemnare "to sentence, doom, blame, disapprove," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + damnare "to harm, damage" (see damn).
1530s, from Middle French commerce (14c.), from Latin commercium "trade, trafficking," from com- "together" (see com-) + merx (genitive mercis) "merchandise" (see market (n.))..
late 14c., "let in," from Latin admittere "to allow to enter, let in, let come, give access," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + mittere "let go, send" (see mission).
Old English unefen "unequal, unlike, anomalous, irregular," from un- (1) "not" + even (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 定义,解释;(轮廓影像等的)清晰度;阐明;

etymology.story: late 14c., "decision, setting of boundaries," from Old French definicion, from Latin definitionem (nominative definitio), noun of action from past participle stem of definire (see define).
1899 in the geological sense, from tectonic (also see -ics); earlier it meant "building or constructive arts in general" (1850)..

meanings.definitionCn: 不受影响的；自然的；真挚的；不矫揉造作的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + 向/朝/在 + 做/制造

etymology.story: 1580s, "not influenced, untouched in mind or feeling," from un- (1) "not" + past participle of affect (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 空 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1670s, in figurative sense, of family trees; 1801 in general sense of "a derivative;" 1814 in literal sense, in reference to plants.
"a lord-master, a protector," c.

type of small songbird, 1650s (short for Canary-bird, 1570s), from French canarie, from Spanish canario "canary bird," literally "of the Canary Islands," from Latin Insula Canaria "Canary Island," largest of the Fortunate Isles, literally "island of dogs" (canis, genitive canarius; see canine (n.)), so called because large dogs lived there.

morphology.mnemonic: in front of, before; end + dat

etymology.story: 1580s, earlier as noun meaning "a backdating, false early date attached to a document or event" (1570s); from Latin ante "before" (see ante) + date (v.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 使需要，必需；承担；遗传给；蕴含

etymology.story: mid-14c., "convert (an estate) into 'fee tail' (feudum talliatum)," from en- (1) "make" + taile "legal limitation," especially of inheritance, ruling who succeeds in ownership and preventing the property from being sold off, from Anglo-French taile, Old French taillie, past participle of taillier "allot, cut to shape," from Late Latin taliare "to split" (see tailor (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 差的；自卑的；下级的，下等的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Late Latin mythicus "legendary," from Greek mythikos, from mythos (see myth)..

meanings.definitionCn: 忽略；俯瞰；远眺；检查；高耸于…之上

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + appearance of a person

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to examine, scrutinize, inspect," from over- + look (v.).

mid-14c., comenden, from Latin commendare "to commit to the care or keeping (of someone), to entrust to; to commit to writing;" hence "to set off, render agreeable, praise," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + mandare "to commit to one's charge" (see mandate (n.)).
"young plant developed from seed," 1650s, from seed (n.) + diminutive suffix -ling..
"to write," mid-15c., from Latin scribere "to write" (see script (n.))..

1580s, from Spanish mosquito "little gnat," diminutive of mosca "fly," from Latin musca "fly," from PIE root *mu- "gnat, fly," imitative of insect buzzing (compare Sanskrit maksa-, Greek myia, Old English mycg, Modern English midge, Old Church Slavonic mucha), perhaps imitative of the sound of humming insects..

1530s, "a hanger-on, a toady, person who lives on others," from Middle French parasite (16c.) or directly from Latin parasitus "toady, sponger," and directly from Greek parasitos "one who lives at another's expense, person who eats at the table of another," from noun use of an adjective meaning "feeding beside," from para- "beside" (see para- (1)) + sitos "food," of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 人工的，人造的，人为的；虚伪的，做作的

morphology.mnemonic: device, trick + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., in the phrase artificial day "part of the day from sunrise to sunset," from Old French artificial, from Latin artificialis "of or belonging to art," from artificium (see artifice).
1630s, from deficience (mid-15c.) + -cy; or from Late Latin deficientia, from deficientem (see deficient)..

1630s, "words used in a ceremony or ritual" (earlier as a Latin word in English), from Latin formula "form, draft, contract, regulation;" in law, "a rule, method;" literally "small form," diminutive of forma "form" (see form (n.)).

1763, from French avalanche (17c.), from Romansch (Swiss) avalantze "descent," altered (by metathesis of -l- and -v-, probably influenced by Old French avaler "to descend, go down") from Savoy dialect lavantse, from Provençal lavanca "avalanche," perhaps from a pre-Latin Alpine language (the suffix -anca suggests Ligurian).

meanings.definitionCn: 注释； 评论； 意见

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French commenter (15c.), from Latin commentari, from commentum (see comment (n.)).
also pass-book, 1828, from pass (v.) + book (n.); apparently the notion is of the document "passing" between bank and customer..

meanings.definitionCn: 戒酒；节欲；（气候等的）温和

root.meaning: degree of hardness and resiliency in steel

morphology.mnemonic: degree of hardness and resiliency in steel + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., "self-restraint, moderation," from Anglo-French temperaunce (mid-13c.), from Latin temperantia "moderation, sobriety, discretion, self-control," from temperans, present participle of temperare "to moderate" (see temper (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 礼物；[税收] 贡物；颂词；（尤指对死者的）致敬，悼念，吊唁礼物

etymology.story: mid-14c., "stated sum of money or other valuable consideration paid by one ruler or country to another in acknowledgment of submission or as the price of peace or protection," from Anglo-French tribute, Old French tribut and directly from Latin tributum "tribute, a stated payment, a thing contributed or paid," noun use of neuter of tributus, past participle of tribuere "to pay, assign, grant," also "allot among the tribes or to a tribe," from tribus (see tribe).
late 15c., from en- (1) "make, put in" + danger.

Old English stemn, stefn "stem of a plant, trunk of a tree," also "either end-post of a ship," from Proto-Germanic *stamniz (source also of Old Saxon stamm, Old Norse stafn "stem of a ship;" Danish stamme, Swedish stam "trunk of a tree;" Old High German stam, German Stamm), from suffixed form of PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

1727, a physician's term for "condition of feeling healthy and comfortable (especially when sick)," medical Latin, from Greek euphoria "power of enduring easily," from euphoros, literally "bearing well," from eu "well" (see eu-) + pherein "to carry" (see infer).
early 15c., "overcharge, charge too much expense," from Old French surcharger "to overload, overburden, overcharge" (12c.), from sur- "over" (see sur- (1)) + chargier "to load" (see charge (v.)).

early 15c., from Middle French oblique (14c.) and directly from Latin obliquus "slanting, sidelong, indirect," from ob "against" (see ob-) + root of licinus "bent upward," from PIE root *lei- "to bend, be movable" (see limb (n.1)).
mid-14c., from profitable + -ity..

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + 顺序/等级 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1755, "fickleness," from versatile + -ity.
late 14c., "exercise of the power of reason; act or process of thinking logically;" also "an instance of this;" verbal noun from reason (v.)..

1640s, a back-formation from pugnacity or else from Latin pugnacis, genitive of pugnax "combative, fond of fighting," from pugnare "to fight," especially with the fists, "contend against," from pugnus "a fist," from PIE *pung-, nasalized form of root *peuk-, *peug- "to stick, stab, to prick" (source also of Greek pyx "with clenched fist," pygme "fist, boxing," pyktes "boxer;" Latin pungere "to pierce, prick")..
also rag-time, "syncopated, jazzy piano music," 1897, perhaps from rag "dance ball" (1895, American English dialect), or a shortening of ragged, in reference to the syncopated melody.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + volve + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., originally of celestial bodies, from Old French revolucion "course, revolution (of celestial bodies)" (13c.), or directly from Late Latin revolutionem (nominative revolutio) "a revolving," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin revolvere "turn, roll back" (see revolve).

1610s, from Italian balcone, from balco "scaffold," from a Germanic source (perhaps Langobardic *balko- "beam," cognate with Old English balca "beam, ridge;" see balk (n.)) + Italian augmentative suffix -one.

also instil, early 15c., "to introduce (liquid, feelings, etc.) little by little," from Latin instillare "put in by drops; to drop, trickle," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + stilla "a drop" (see distill).
1610s, "to take in as food," from Latin ingestus, past participle of ingerere "to throw in, pour in, heap upon," from in- "into" (see in- (2)) + gerere "to carry" (see gest).
1560s, raliabill, Scottish; see rely + -able.
1660s, "good-tasting," from palate + -able.

meanings.definitionCn: 兼容的；能共处的；可并立的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French compatible (15c.), from Medieval Latin compatibilis, from Late Latin compati (see compassion).

1580s, "one who passes judgment," from Middle French critique (14c.), from Latin criticus "a judge, literary critic," from Greek kritikos "able to make judgments," from krinein "to separate, decide" (see crisis).

meanings.definitionCn: 违反规定，反常；撤消管制规定

etymology.story: 1898, loan-translation of German Photosynthese, from photo- "light" (see photo-) + synthese "synthesis" (see synthesis).

meanings.definitionCn: 拒绝；否定；批判；与…断绝关系；拒付

etymology.story: 1540s, "to cast off by divorce," from Latin repudiatus, past participle of repudiare "to cast off, put away, divorce, reject, scorn, disdain," from repudium "divorce, rejection, a putting away, dissolution of marriage," from re- "back, away" (see re-) + pudium, which is probably related to pes/ped- "foot" [Barnhart].

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + limin + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1660s, from French préliminaire and directly from Medieval Latin praeliminaris, from Latin prae- "before" (see pre-) + limen (genitive liminis) "threshold" (see limit (n.)).
early 14c., "bad luck, unlucky accident," from mis- (1) "bad" + hap "luck." Probably on analogy of Old French meschance (see mischance (n.))..

mid-14c., from Old French obstacle, ostacle "opposition, obstruction, hindrance" (13c.) or directly from Latin obstaculum "a hindrance, obstacle," with instrumental suffix *-tlom + obstare "stand before, stand opposite to, block, hinder, thwart," from ob "against" (see ob-) + stare "to stand," from PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

morphology.mnemonic: habitual diligence, effort + 与…有关的/形容词 + ize + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1883, noun of action from industrialize (q.v.)..
late 14c., "provide with a crest," from Old French crester, from creste (see crest (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 看见 + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "as a temporary arrangement for the present," c.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + device for pressing cloth
出/向外/前任 + 取/抓 + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1828, from exception + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 建立/放置

etymology.story: mid-15c., verb use of adjective constitute, "made up, formed" (late 14c.), from Latin constitutus "arranged, settled," past participle adjective from constituere "to cause to stand, set up, fix, place, establish, set in order; form something new; resolve," of persons, "to appoint to an office," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + statuere "to set," from PIE root *stā- "to stand," with derivatives meaning "place or thing that is standing" (see stet).
1839, noun of action from ornament (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 用基准问题测试（计算机系统等）

etymology.story: also bench-mark, "surveyor's point of reference," 1838, from a specialized surveyors' use of bench (n.) + mark (n.1); figurative sense is from 1884..

early 15c., from Old French éminent "prominent" (13c.) or directly from Latin eminentem (nominative eminens) "standing out, projecting, prominent, high," figuratively "distinguished, distinctive," present participle of eminere "stand out, project; be prominent, be conspicuous," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + -minere, which is related to mons "hill" (see mount (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 站立/设置 + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French resistance, earlier resistence, from Late Latin resistentia, from present participle stem of Latin resistere "make a stand against, oppose" (see resist).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + equi + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: early 15c., "difference of rank or dignity," from Old French inequalité (14c., Modern French inégalité) and directly from Medieval Latin inaequalitas, from Latin inaequalis "unequal, unlike, different (in size); changeable, inconstant," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + aequalis "equal" (see equal).

meanings.definitionCn: (旅行)服务员；信使；送急件的人

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Anglo-French courrier, from Old French coreor, ultimately an agent noun from Latin currere "to run" (see current (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 谱，光谱，频谱；范围，幅度，系列

etymology.story: 1610s, "apparition, specter," from Latin spectrum (plural spectra) "an appearance, image, apparition, specter," from specere "to look at, view" (see scope (n.1)).
1520s, "to overcome in argument," from Latin convincere "to overcome decisively," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + vincere "to conquer" (see victor).

meanings.definitionCn: 刺激的； 使人愤怒的， 气人的

morphology.mnemonic: 激怒/刺激 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "that causes annoyance," 1707, present participle adjective from irritate (v.).
"one who 'drops out' of something," 1930, from drop (v.) + out (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 陈旧的， 过时的； 废弃的

etymology.story: 1620s, past participle adjective from antiquate (1530s) "to make old or obsolete," from Latin antiquatus, past participle of antiquare (see antique (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 怪癖， 癖性； 过分的独特风格

morphology.mnemonic: customary practice + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: "excessive use of distinctive methods in art or literature," 1803, from manner + -ism.
late 14c., contraction of remenant (c.

1540s, "one who enters into a contract," from Late Latin contractor, agent noun from past participle stem of Latin contrahere (see contract (n.)); specifically of "one who enters into a contract to provide work, services, or goods" from 1724..

meanings.definitionCn: 柴油； 柴油机； 柴油车

etymology.story: 1894, named for Rudolf Diesel (1858-1913), German mechanical engineer who designed this type of engine..

meanings.definitionCn: 反对；抨击；轻视；声明不赞成

etymology.story: 1620s, "to pray against or for deliverance from," from Latin deprecatus, past participle of deprecari "to pray (something) away" (see deprecation).

meanings.definitionCn: 陡峭的；不合理的；夸大的；急剧升降的

etymology.story: "to soak in a liquid," early 14c., of uncertain origin, originally in reference to barley or malt, probably cognate with Old Norse steypa "to pour out, throw" (perhaps from an unrecorded Old English cognate), from Proto-Germanic *staupijanan.

morphology.mnemonic: 标记 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: 1530s, a kind of document in Scottish law, from Middle French signature (16c.) or directly from Medieval Latin signatura "signature, a rescript," in classical Latin "the matrix of a seal," from signatus, past participle of signare "to mark with a stamp, sign" (see sign (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 共和国的；共和政体的；共和主义的；拥护共和政体的

etymology.story: "one who favors a republic or republican principles" (or, as Johnson puts it, "One who thinks a commonwealth without monarchy the best government"), 1690s; see republican (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 建筑师；设计师；缔造者；创造者

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French architecte, from Latin architectus, from Greek arkhitekton "master builder, director of works," from arkhi- "chief" (see archon) + tekton "builder, carpenter" (see texture).

root.meaning: wrong, not in accord with what is accepted

morphology.mnemonic: wrong, not in accord with what is accepted + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of turning aside from truth, corruption, distortion" (originally of religious beliefs), from Latin perversionem (nominative perversio) "a turning about," noun of action from past participle stem of pervertere (see pervert (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 寿衣；覆盖物；船的横桅索；[电] 护罩

etymology.story: "one who acts in place of another," early 15c., from Middle French substitut (noun use) and directly from Latin substitutus, past participle of substituere "put in place of another" (see substitution).

meanings.definitionCn: 吸引人的花招， 噱头

etymology.story: 1910, American English, perhaps an alteration of gimcrack, or an anagram of magic..
1540s, from Middle French amplitude or directly from Latin amplitudinem (nominative amplitudo) "wide extent, width," from amplus (see ample).

meanings.definitionCn: 伪造；篡改；歪曲；证明虚假

etymology.story: mid-15c., "to prove false," from Middle French falsifier (15c.), from Late Latin falsificare "make false, corrupt," from Latin falsus "erroneous, mistaken" (see false).

meanings.definitionCn: 身份；同一性，一致；特性；恒等式

morphology.mnemonic: in + stall + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "action of installing," in reference to church offices or other positions, from Medieval Latin installationem (nominative installatio), noun of action from past participle stem of installare (see install).

meanings.definitionCn: 联邦的；同盟的；联邦政府的； 联邦制的

morphology.mnemonic: 联盟 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1640s, as a theological term (in reference to "covenants" between God and man), from French fédéral, an adjective formed from Latin foedus (genitive foederis) "covenant, league, treaty, alliance," from PIE *bhoid-es-, from root *bheidh- "to trust" (which also is the source of Latin fides "faith;" see faith).

morphology.mnemonic: 暴力/违反 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to break" (an oath, etc.), from Latin violatus, past participle of violare "treat with violence, dishonor, outrage" (see violation).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 群体 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., "a gathering, assembly," from Old French congregacion (12c., Modern French congrégation), from Latin congregationem (nominative congregatio), noun of action from congregare (see congregate).

early 14c., from Old French verifier "substantiate, find out the truth about" (14c.), from Medieval Latin verificare "make true," from Latin verus "true" (see very) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious)..

"young ox," Old English steor "bullock," from Proto-Germanic *steuraz (source also of Old Saxon stior, Old Norse stjorr, Swedish tjur, Danish tyr, Middle Dutch, Dutch, German stier, Gothic stiur "bull"), perhaps from PIE *steu-ro-, denoting "larger domestic animal" (see taurus).

morphology.mnemonic: paid by the recipient + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French collectif, from Latin collectivus, from collectus (see collect).

meanings.definitionCn: 短途旅行,游览;离题;[物理]偏移,漂移

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + to swear profanely + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1570s, "a deviation in argument," also "a military sally," from Latin excursionem (nominative excursio) "a running forth, sally, excursion, expedition," figuratively "an outset, opening," noun of action from past participle stem of excurrere "run out, run forth, hasten forward; project, extend," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + currere "to run" (see current (adj.)).
Old English undergan "obtain, get; undertake," from under + gan (see go (v.)).
"outside certain limits," 1660s, from out + present participle of lie (v.2).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + one's occupation, branch of business + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1550s, from Latin delineatus, past participle of delineare "to sketch out," from de- "completely" (see de-) + lineare "draw lines," from linea "line" (see line (n.)).
early 15c., from by (adv.) + gone.

meanings.definitionCn: 息票；赠券；联票；[经] 配给券

etymology.story: 1822, "certificate of interest due on a bond" (which could be cut from the bond and presented for payment), from French coupon, literally "piece cut off," from couper "to cut," from coup "a blow" (see coup).

meanings.definitionCn: 由铁道运输；铺设铁路；以捏造不实之罪使入狱

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French multitude (12c.) and directly from Latin multitudinem (nominative multitudo) "a great number, a crowd; the crowd, the common people," from multus "many, much" (see multi-) + suffix -tudo (see -tude).
"one of the family of mammals represented by man," 1889, from Modern Latin Hominidæ the biological family name (1825), from Latin homo (genitive hominis) "man" (see homunculus) + -id.

late 14c., from Old French enhabiter, enabiter "dwell in, live in, reside" (12c.), from Latin inhabitare "to dwell in," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + habitare "to dwell," frequentative of habere "hold, have" (see habit (n.)).
1923, from counter- + measure (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 先天的；固有的；与生俱来的

etymology.story: early 15c., "existing from birth," from Late Latin innatus "inborn, native, natural" (source also of French inné, Spanish and Italian innato), past participle of innasci "to be born in, originate in," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + nasci "to be born" (Old Latin gnasci; see genus).

meanings.definitionCn: 围攻；包围；围城；不断袭击；长期努力

etymology.story: early 13c., "a seat" (as in Siege Perilous, early 13c., the vacant seat at Arthur's Round Table, according to prophecy to be occupied safely only by the knight destined to find the Holy Grail), from Old French sege "seat, throne," from Vulgar Latin *sedicum "seat," from Latin sedere "sit" (see sedentary).

1704, "kernel of a nut," 1708, "head of a comet," from Latin nucleus "kernel," from nucula "little nut," diminutive of nux (genitive nucis) "nut," from PIE *kneu- "nut" (source also of Middle Irish cnu, Welsh cneuen, Middle Breton knoen "nut," Old Norse hnot, Old English hnutu "nut").
Old English stincg, steng "act of stinging, puncture, thrust," from the root of sting (v.).
1540s, from Latin abstractus or else from the adjective abstract.
1802, from Latin alluvius "alluvial" (see alluvium) + -al (1)..

meanings.definitionCn: 恒星状球体, 类星体

etymology.story: 1964, from "quas(i-stell)ar radio source" (1963); from quasi- + stellar..

mid-15c., "condition of being a father," from Old French paternité (12c.), from Late Latin paternitatem (nominative paternitas) "fatherly care, fatherhood," from Latin paternus "of a father," from pater (see father (n.)).

etymology.story: Old English spannan "to join, link, clasp, fasten, bind, connect; stretch, span," from Proto-Germanic *spannan (source also of Old Norse spenna, Old Frisian spanna, Middle Dutch spannen, Dutch spannan "stretch, bend, hoist, hitch," Old High German spannan, German spannen "to join, fasten, extend, connect"), from PIE root *(s)pen- "to draw, stretch, spin" (source also of Latin pendere "to hang, to cause to hang," pondus "weight" (perhaps the notion is the weight of a thing measured by how much it stretches a cord), pensare "to weigh, consider;" Greek ponos "toil," ponein "to toil;" Lithuanian spendziu "lay a snare;" Old Church Slavonic peti "stretch, strain," pato "fetter," pina "I span;" Old English spinnan "to spin;" for other cognates, see spin (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + bring or keep in equilibrium

etymology.story: 1895, from im- "not" + balance (n.)..

etymology.story: 1640s, from French bizarre "odd, fantastic" (16c.), from Italian bizarro "irascible, tending to quick flashes of anger" (13c.), from bizza "fit of anger, quick flash of anger" (13c.).

morphology.mnemonic: 星 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

early 15c., from Middle French fertilité, from Latin fertilitatem (nominative fertilitas) "fruitfulness, fertility," from fertilis "fruitful, productive" (see fertile)..

meanings.definitionCn: 入口；门槛；开始；极限；临界值

etymology.story: Old English þrescold, þærscwold, þerxold, etc., "door-sill, point of entering," of uncertain origin and probably much altered by folk-etymology.

1849, back-formation from plural form termites (1781), from Modern Latin termites (three syllables), plural of termes (genitive termitis), a special use of Late Latin termes "woodworm, white ant," altered (by influence of Latin terere "to rub, wear, erode") from earlier Latin tarmes.
"head," early 14c., polle "hair of the head; piece of fur from the head of an animal," also "head," from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch pol "head, top." Sense extended early 14c.

etymology.story: 1530s, "condition of being smaller," from Middle French minorité (15c.), or directly from Medieval Latin minoritatem (nominative minoritas), from Latin minor (see minor (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 严格的， 苛刻的； 紧缩的， 短缺的， 银根紧的； 迫切的

morphology.mnemonic: 拉紧/约束 + …的人/处于…状态

把…关在室内；装填（弹药等）
公文包；文件夹； 作品集；投资组合

etymology.story: 1722, porto folio; 1719 as port folio, from Italian portafoglio "a case for carrying loose papers," from porta, imperative of portare "to carry" (see port (n.1)) + foglio "sheet, leaf," from Latin folium (see folio).
1510s, "escape," from Middle French evader, from Latin evadere "to escape, get away," from assimilated form of ex- "away" (see ex-) + vadere "to go, walk" (see vamoose).

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/离开 + 群体 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1550s, "act of segregating," from Late Latin segregationem (nominative segregatio), noun of action from past participle stem of segregare (see segregate).

mid-13c., "part from each other," from Old French departir (10c.) "to divide, distribute; separate (oneself), depart; die," from Late Latin departire "divide" (transitive), from de- "from" (see de-) + partire "to part, divide," from pars (genitive partis) "a part" (see part (n.)).

1530s, "to pile up, accumulate," from Latin exaggeratus, past participle of exaggerare "heighten, amplify, magnify," literally "to heap, pile, load, fill," from ex- "thoroughly" (see ex-) + aggerare "heap up, accumulate," figuratively "amplify, magnify," from agger (genitive aggeris) "heap," from aggerere "bring together, carry toward," from assimilated form of ad- "to, toward" (see ad-) + gerere "carry" (see gest).
1650s, "outer shell, husk," from Latin cortex "bark of a tree" (see corium).

morphology.mnemonic: set up a loud cry, + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French exploration and directly from Latin explorationem (nominative exploratio) "an examination," noun of action from past participle stem of explorare "investigate, examine" (see explore).
late 14c., implien, emplien "to enfold, enwrap, entangle" (the classical Latin sense), from Old French emplier, from Latin implicare "involve, enfold, entangle" (see implication).

meanings.definitionCn: 抵消，补偿；平版印刷；支管
抵消；弥补；用平版印刷术印刷

meanings.definitionCn: 委托； 许诺； 承担义务

meanings.definitionCn: 化石；僵化的事物；顽固不化的人

etymology.story:
1610s, "any thing dug up;" 1650s (adj.) "obtained by digging" (of coal, salt, etc.), from French fossile (16c.), from Latin fossilis "dug up," from fossus, past participle of fodere "to dig," from PIE root *bhedh- "to dig, pierce."

Restricted noun sense of "geological remains of a plant or animal" is from 1736 (the adjective in the sense "pertaining to fossils" is from 1660s); slang meaning "old person" first recorded 1859.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 投/掷 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1550s, from French réjection (16c.) or directly from Latin reiectionem (nominative reiectio) "act of throwing back," noun of action from past participle stem of reicere (see reject).
Old English discipul (fem.

etymology.story: 1590s, "act of fixing," perhaps from fix (v.) on model of mixture, or from an assumed Latin *fixitatem.

morphology.mnemonic: 狂热 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French combustion (13c.), from Latin combustionem (nominative combustio) "a burning," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin comburere "to burn up, consume," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + *burere, faulty separation of amburere "to burn around," actually ambi-urere, from urere "to burn, singe," from PIE root *eus- "to burn" (see ember)..
"inflammation of a joint," 1540s, from medical Latin arthritis, from Greek (nosos) arthritis "(disease) of the joints," from arthritis, fem.

etymology.story: early 15c., "intercession, intercessory prayer," from Middle French intervention or directly from Late Latin interventionem (nominative interventio) "an interposing, a giving security," literally "a coming between," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin intervenire "to come between, interrupt," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + venire "come" (see venue).

meanings.definitionCn: 更换；复位；代替者；补充兵员

etymology.story: 1550s, from Latin configurationem (nominative configuratio), noun of action from past participle stem of configurare (see configure)..
"act on each other, act reciprocally," 1805, from inter- + act (v.).
1590s, from French bombarder, from bombarde "mortar, catapult" (see bombard (n.)).
early 14c., bistowen "give" (as alms, etc.), from be- + stowen "to place" (see stow).

1802, coined probably by German-born English astronomer William Herschel (1738-1822) from Greek asteroeides "star-like," from aster "star" (see astro-) + -eidos "form, shape" (see -oid)..

meanings.definitionCn: 普通的n.寻常的事物，平庸的东西

etymology.story: 1540s, "a statement generally accepted," literal translation of Latin locus communis, from Greek koinos topos "general topic." See common (adj.) + place (n.).
"number of persons, things, etc., regarded as a unit," early 15c., from noun use of Latin adjective aggregatum, neuter of aggregatus (see aggregate (adj.))..

morphology.mnemonic: capt + 有…倾向的 + 使…/具有…的

1785, from French provenance "origin, production," from provenant, present participle of Middle French provenir "come forth, arise, originate," from Latin provenire "come forth, originate, appear, arise," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + venire "come" (see venue)..

meanings.definitionCn: 数组，阵列；排列，列阵；大批，一系列；衣服

early 15c., "extinguished, quenched," from Latin extinctus/exstinctus, past participle of extinguere/exstinguere "to put out, quench; go out, die out; kill, destroy" (see extinguish).

etymology.story: early 15c., opake, from Latin opacus "shaded, in the shade, shady, dark, darkened, obscure," of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 判定/控告 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1890, from Latin stem of criminal + -ology.

early 15c., from Old French humide, umide "damp, wet" (15c.) or directly from Latin humidus "moist, wet," variant (probably by influence of humus "earth") of umidus, from umere "be moist, be wet," from Proto-Italic *umo- "wet" (also source of Latin umidus "wet, moist," umiditas "moisture," umor "moisture, fluid," umectus "moist, wet"), perhaps from PIE *uhrmo- "wet," from the same source as Latin urina [de Vaan]..

late 14c., from Old French controversie or directly from Latin controversia, from controversus "turned in an opposite direction, disputed, turned against," from contra- "against" (see contra) + versus (see verse)..

meanings.definitionCn: 变量的；可变的；易变的，多变的；变异的，[生物] 畸变的
[数] 变量；可变物，可变因素

1580s, from mis- (1) + interpret.

"lasting only for a time," 1540s, from Latin temporarius "of seasonal character, lasting a short time," from tempus (genitive temporis) "time, season" (see temporal, late 14c., which was the earlier word for "lasting but for a time").

"area or region where a plant or animal naturally grows or lives," 1762, originally a technical term in Latin texts on English flora and fauna, literally "it inhabits," third person singular present indicative of habitare "to live, inhabit, dwell," frequentative of habere "to have, to hold, possess" (see habit (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 眼/视 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "most favorable," 1890, from Latin optimus (see optimum).

mid-15c., from Latin resplendentem (nominative resplendens) "brilliant, radiant," present participle of resplendere "to glitter, shine," from re-, intensive prefix, + splendere "to shine, be splendid" (see splendid).
1660s, from accurate + -cy..

morphology.mnemonic: 地球 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1791, from geology + -ical.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 打破 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1570s, from Late Latin refractionem (nominative refractio) "a breaking up," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin refringere "to break up," from re- "back" (see re-) + comb.
1660s, from Medieval Latin quota, from Latin quota pars "how large a part," from quota, fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 热 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1756, "having to do with hot springs," from French thermal (Buffon), from Greek therme "heat, feverish heat," from PIE *gwher- "to heat, warm" (source also of Latin fornax "an oven, kiln," formus "warm," Old English wearm; see warm (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 看见 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "pertaining to the faculty of sight;" also "coming from the eye or sight" (as a beam of light was thought to do), from Late Latin visualis "of sight," from Latin visus "a sight, a looking; power of sight; things seen, appearance," from visus, past participle of videre "to see" (see vision).
1620s (adjective and noun), from Greek diagnostikos "able to distinguish," from diagnostos, verbal adjective from diagignoskein (see diagnosis).

1727, "ground for training horses," from French terrain "piece of earth, ground, land," from Old French (12c.), from Vulgar Latin *terranum, from Latin terrenum "land, ground," noun use of neuter of terrenus "of earth, earthly," from terra "earth, land," literally "dry land" (as opposed to "sea"); from PIE root *ters- "to dry" (source also of Sanskrit tarsayati "dries up," Avestan tarshu- "dry, solid," Greek teresesthai "to become or be dry," Latin torrere "dry up, parch," Gothic þaursus "dry, barren," Old High German thurri, German dürr, Old English þyrre "dry;" Old English þurstig "thirsty").

meanings.definitionCn: 圣堂， 圣殿， 圣坛； 圣地； 庇护所， 避难所； 禁猎区， 动物保护区

etymology.story: early 14c., "building set apart for holy worship," from Anglo-French sentuarie, Old French saintuaire "sacred relic, holy thing; reliquary, sanctuary," from Late Latin sanctuarium "a sacred place, shrine" (especially the Hebrew Holy of Holies; see sanctum), also "a private room," from Latin sanctus "holy" (see saint (n.)).

early 15c., "to tell, inform, impart knowledge or information," also "furnish with authoritative directions," from Latin instructus, past participle of instruere "arrange, prepare, set in order; inform, teach," literally "to build, erect," from in- "on" (see in- (2)) + struere "to pile, build" (see structure (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + portion + 与…有关的/形容词
经济/管理 + 具有…特征/名词化

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

meanings.definitionCn: 同一地区全体居民,社会,社区;共同体

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French comunité "community, commonness, everybody" (Modern French communauté), from Latin communitatem (nominative communitas) "community, society, fellowship, friendly intercourse; courtesy, condescension, affability," from communis "common, public, general, shared by all or many," (see common (adj.)).

1580s, "a translation," from Middle French version, from Medieval Latin versionem (nominative versio) "a turning, a translation," from past participle stem of Latin vertere "to turn, change, alter, translate" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 使资本化；以大写字母写；估计…的价值

morphology.mnemonic: capit + 与…有关的/形容词 + ize

1640s, literally, 1726 figuratively, from French bronzer (16c.) or else from bronze (n.).
early 15c., "impervious to water," from Old French estanche "firm, watertight," fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 市(立,政)的;地方性的,地方自治的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French municipal, from Latin municipalis "of a citizen of a free town, of a free town," also "of a petty town, provincial," from municipium "free town, city whose citizens have the privileges of Roman citizens but are governed by their own laws," from municeps "citizen, inhabitant of a free town." Second element is root of capere "assume, take" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 开始（调查）；制定；创立；提起（诉讼）

etymology.story: 1510s, "purpose, design," from Latin institutum "an ordinance; a purpose; a custom; precedents; principal components," literally "thing set up," noun use of neuter past participle of instituere "to set up, put in place; arrange; found, establish" (see institute (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 适当的恰当的特有的vt.拨给,挪用,盗用

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + propri + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "specially suitable, proper," early 15c., from Latin appropriatus, past participle of appropriare (see appropriate (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 内涵；含蓄；暗示，隐含意义；储蓄的东西（词、语等）

etymology.story: 1530s, from Medieval Latin connotationem (nominative connotatio), from connotat-, past participle stem of connotare "signify in addition to the main meaning," a term in logic, literally "to mark along with," from Latin com- "together" (see com-) + notare "to mark" (see note).
1660s, from Latinized form of Greek kriterion "means for judging, standard," from krites "judge," from PIE root *krei- (see crisis).
1610s, "one concerned with finances" (especially public), from French financier (16c.), from finance (see finance (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 初级读本；识字课本；原始物

etymology.story: late 14c., essencia (respelled late 15c.

early 15c., "service book used by a priest," from Old French manuel "handbook" (also "plow-handle"), from Late Latin manuale "case or cover of a book, handbook," noun use of neuter of Latin manualis (see manual (adj.)).

late 14c., "uterus, womb," from Old French matrice "womb, uterus," from Latin matrix (genitive matricis) "pregnant animal," in Late Latin "womb," also "source, origin," from mater (genitive matris) "mother" (see mother (n.1)).
1838, American English, in reference to traffic in slaves, from inter- "between" + state (n.) in the U.S.

meanings.definitionCn: 雄辩的,有说服力的;善辩的,口才流利的

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 说话 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French eloquent, from Latin eloquentem (nominative eloquens) "speaking, having the faculty of speech; eloquent," present participle of eloqui "to speak out" (see eloquence).

root.meaning: to attend to in practice, to keep, follow

morphology.mnemonic: to attend to in practice, to keep, follow + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: "building for observing astronomical phenomena," 1670s (in reference to Greenwich), from French observatoire, from observer (v.); see observe..

meanings.definitionCn: 支持者；建议者；提出认证遗嘱者

etymology.story: 1580s, "one who brings forth a proposition or argument," from Latin proponentem (nominative proponens), present participle of proponere "put forward" (see propound).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之下 + collective shipping of a country

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Anglo-French cupidite, Middle French cupidité, from Latin cupiditatem (nominative cupiditas) "passionate desire, lust; ambition," from cupidus "eager, passionate," from cupere "to desire" (perhaps cognate with Sanskrit kupyati "bubbles up, becomes agitated," Old Church Slavonic kypeti "to boil," Lithuanian kupeti "to boil over").

meanings.definitionCn: 样子, 外表, 面貌, (问题等的)方面

1839, irregular formation from vegetable (n.) + -arian, as in agrarian, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 粒子,微粒;极小量;小品词,虚词

1530s, "attack, assault," from on + set (n.); compare verbal phrase to set (something) on (someone).
1650s, "exposition of principles," from Late Latin rationale, noun use of neuter of Latin rationalis "of reason" (see rational).

early 14c., "open to view," from Old French overt (Modern French ouvert), past participle of ovrir "to open," from Latin aperire "to open, uncover," from PIE compound *ap-wer-yo- from *ap- "off, away" (see abo-) + base *wer- (5) "to cover" (see weir).

1510s, "refuse, reject," from Middle French réfuter (16c.) and directly from Latin refutare "drive back; rebut, disprove; repress, repel, resist, oppose," from re- "back" (see re-) + -futare "to beat," probably from PIE root *bhau- "to strike down" (see bat (n.1)).

Old English also used tweo "two" to mean "doubt." Compare doubt (v.).

1620s, from Latin collidere "strike together," from com- "together" (see com-) + laedere "to strike, injure by striking," which is of unknown origin.

mid-15c., from Anglo-French propicius, Old French propicius "gracious, favorable, useful" (12c., Modern French propice) and directly from Latin propitius "favorable, kind, gracious, well-disposed" (see propitiation).

"stone in small, irregular fragments," early 13c., from Old French gravele "sand, gravel; sea-shore; sandy bed of a river," diminutive of grave "sand, seashore" (Modern French grève), possibly from Celtic *graw- (compare Welsh gro "coarse gravel," Breton grouan, Cornish grow "gravel"), perhaps ultimately from PIE *ghreu- "to rub, grind" (see grit (n.)).

1560s, "one who nourishes or rears;" 1670s, "one who trains or instructs," from Latin educator (in classical Latin, "a foster father," then also "a tutor"), agent noun from past participle stem of educare (see educate).

early 15c., "sale of commodities in small quantities or parcels or at second hand" (opposed to wholesale), from Old French retail "piece cut off, shred, scrap, paring" (Modern French retaille), from retaillier (see retail (v.)).

Old English wiðstandan "resist, oppose," from wið "against" (see with) + standan "to stand" (see stand (v.)); perhaps a loan-translation of Latin resistere "to resist" (see resist).

1716, earlier aera (1610s), from Late Latin aera, era "an era or epoch from which time is reckoned" (7c.), probably identical with Latin aera "counters used for calculation," plural of aes (genitive aeris) "brass, copper, money" (see ore, also compare copper).
Old English gield "payment, sum of money; service, offering, worship;" from the source of yield (v.).

late 14c., administren, aministren "to manage as a steward," from Old French amenistrer "help, aid, be of service to" (12c., Modern French administrer, the -d- restored 16c.), and directly from Latin administrare "to help, assist; manage, control, guide, superintend; rule, direct," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + ministrare "serve" (see minister (v.)).

etymology.story:
"cast off," Old English sceadan, scadan "to divide, separate, part company; discriminate, decide; scatter abroad, cast about," strong verb (past tense scead, past participle sceadan), from Proto-Germanic *skaithan (source also of Old Saxon skethan, Old Frisian sketha, Middle Dutch sceiden, Dutch scheiden, Old High German sceidan, German scheiden "part, separate, distinguish," Gothic skaidan "separate"), from *skaith "divide, split."

According to Klein's sources, this probably is related to PIE root *skei- "to cut, separate, divide, part, split" (source also of Sanskrit chid-, Greek skhizein, Latin scindere "to split;" Lithuanian skedzu "I make thin, separate, divide;" Old Irish scian "knife;" Welsh chwydu "to break open").
1794 (transitive), from fossil + -ize.

"in, within" + gignere (perfective genui) "beget," from PIE root *gene- "to produce, give birth, beget" (see genus).
mid-15c., "removal from office or position," from Medieval Latin deprivationem (nominative deprivatio), noun of action from past participle stem of deprivare (see deprive)..

etymology.story: late 14c., "to take upon oneself, to take liberty," also "to take for granted, presuppose," especially overconfidently, from Old French presumer (12c.) and directly from Latin praesumere "anticipate," in Late Latin, "assume" (see presumption).

1690s, "desk with drawers, writing desk," from French bureau "office; desk, writing table," originally "cloth covering for a desk," from burel "coarse woolen cloth" (as a cover for writing desks), Old French diminutive of bure "dark brown cloth," which is perhaps either from Latin burrus "red," or from Late Latin burra "wool, shaggy garment." Offices being full of such desks, the meaning expanded 1720 to "division of a government." Meaning "chest of drawers" is from 1770, said to be American English but early in British use..

early 13c., "trunk or projecting nose of an animal," from Middle Low German and Middle Dutch snute "snout," from Proto-Germanic *snut- (source also of German Schnauze, Norwegian snut, Danish snude "snout"), which Watkins traces to a hypothetical Germanic root *snu- forming words having to do with the nose, imitative of a sudden drawing of breath (compare Old English gesnot "nasal mucus;" German schnauben "pant, puff, snort" (Austrian dialect), schnaufen "breathe heavily, pant," Schnupfen "cold in the head;" Old Norse snaldr "snout" (of a serpent), snuthra "to sniff, snuffle").
