etymology.story: 1540s (perhaps early 15c.), from Old French analogie or directly from Latin analogia, from Greek analogia "proportion," from ana- "upon, according to" (see ana-) + logos "ratio," also "word, speech, reckoning" (see logos).

meanings.definitionCn: 吸引，占用；使参加；雇佣；使订婚；预定

etymology.story: early 15c., "to pledge" (something, as security for payment), from Old French engagier "bind (by promise or oath), pledge; pawn" (12c.), from phrase en gage "under pledge," from en "in" (see en- (1)) + gage "pledge," through Frankish from Proto-Germanic *wadiare "pledge" (see wed).

meanings.definitionCn: 日常工作；琐碎烦人的杂务

etymology.story: 1751, American English, variant of char, from Middle English cherre "odd job," from Old English cerr, cierr "turn, change, time, occasion, affair business.".

meanings.definitionCn: 食欲，胃口；欲望，性欲；爱好，趣味

etymology.story: 1810, "art of a general," from French stratégie (18c.) and directly from Greek strategia "office or command of a general," from strategos "general, commander of an army," also the title of various civil officials and magistrates, from stratos "multitude, army, expedition, encamped army," literally "that which is spread out" (see structure (n.)) + agos "leader," from agein "to lead" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 余数，残余；剩余物；其余的人

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French remainder, Old French remaindre, noun use of infinitive, a variant of Old French remanoir (see remain (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 低于/不足 + thes + 过程/状态

etymology.story: 1590s, "a particular statement;" 1650s, "a proposition, assumed and taken for granted, used as a premise," from Middle French hypothese and directly from Late Latin hypothesis, from Greek hypothesis "base, groundwork, foundation," hence in extended use "basis of an argument, supposition," literally "a placing under," from hypo- "under" (see hypo-) + thesis "a placing, proposition" (see thesis).

meanings.definitionCn: 领土，领域；范围；地域；版图

etymology.story: late 14c., "land under the jurisdiction of a town, state, etc.," probably from Latin territorium "land around a town, domain, district," from terra "earth, land" (see terrain) + -orium, suffix denoting place (see -ory).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 抓住/理解

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to understand," from Latin comprehendere "to take together, to unite; include; seize" (of catching fire or the arrest of criminals); also "to comprehend, perceive" (to seize or take in the mind), from com- "completely" (see com-) + prehendere "to catch hold of, seize" (see prehensile).

1590s, "born out of wedlock," from Latin spurius "illegitimate, false" (source also of Italian spurio, Spanish espurio), from spurius (n.) "illegitimate child," probably from Etruscan spural "public." Sense of "having an irregular origin, not properly constituted" is from c.

meanings.definitionCn: 正确无误的；准确的，精确的

etymology.story: 1610s, "done with care," from Latin accuratus "prepared with care, exact, elaborate," past participle of accurare "take care of," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + curare "take care of" (see cure (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 走/让步 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French concession (14c.) or directly from Latin concessionem (nominative concessio) "an allowing, conceding," noun of action from past participle stem of concedere (see concede).

morphology.mnemonic: day日 + 具有…特征/名词化
在…之前/预先 + 不/分离 + 放置 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1620s, from pre- + disposition..

meanings.definitionCn: 专门知识；专门技术；专家的意见

etymology.story: "quality or state of being an expert," 1868, from French expertise (16c.) "expert appraisal, expert's report," from expert (see expert).
1580s, from Middle French bataillon (16c.), from Italian battaglione "battle squadron," from diminutive of Vulgar Latin battalia "battle," from Latin bauttere "to beat" (see batter (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 专业的；职业的；职业性的

morphology.mnemonic: declare openly + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词
greatest premise, greatest among propositions + ize

etymology.story: 1802, formed in English from maximum + -ize; first attested in Bentham, who used it often.
"deeply rooted," 1590s, literally "dyed with grain "cochineal," the red dyestuff (see engrain).

meanings.definitionCn: 选举的，有选举权的；可任意选择的

etymology.story: early 15c., "voluntary," from Late Latin electivus, from elect-, past participle stem of eligere "to pick out, choose" (see election).

morphology.mnemonic: 相同/识别 + 使…化/造成 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1640s, "treating of a thing as the same as another; act of making or proving to be the same," from French identification, probably from identifier (see identify).

1844, from French protéine, coined 1838 by Dutch chemist Gerhard Johan Mulder (1802-1880), perhaps on suggestion of Berzelius, from Greek proteios "the first quality," from protos "first" (see proto-) + -ine (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 难以置信的，不像真实的

etymology.story: "not having an appearance of truth or credibility," 1670s, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + plausible.

meanings.definitionCn: 凭借，手段；度假胜地；常去之地
求助，诉诸；常去；采取某手段或方法

morphology.mnemonic: 见证/证明 + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: late 14c., "give legal testimony, affirm the truth of, bear witness to;" of things, c.

1590s, apparently diminutive and frequentative of Middle English dwinen "waste away, fade, vanish," from Old English dwinan, from Proto-Germanic *dwinan (source also of Dutch dwijnen "to vanish," Old Norse dvina, Danish tvine, Low German dwinen), from PIE *dheu- (3) "to die" (see die (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 分离， 游离， 分裂

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + socio + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1610s (implied in dissociated), from Latin dissociatus, past participle of dissociare "to separate from companionship, disunite, set at variance," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + sociare "to join," from socius "companion" (see social (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 包含； 包围； 环绕

etymology.story: 1550s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + compass (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 页边空白;边缘;余地;幅度

etymology.story: also mega-city, 1968, from mega- + city..
1520s (originally figurative), from pregnant (adj.1) + -cy.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1911, from German Allergie, coined 1906 by  Austrian pediatrician Clemens E.

meanings.definitionCn: 精炼,精制,提纯;使文雅,使高尚

etymology.story: late 14c., from adjective or genitive form of Old English æspe "aspen tree, white poplar," from Proto-Germanic *aspo- (source also of Old Norse ösp, Middle Dutch espe, Old High German aspa, German Espe), from PIE *apsa "aspen" (source also of Lithuanian opuse).

meanings.definitionCn: 增加；爬上；使骑上马；安装，架置；镶嵌，嵌入；准备上演；成立（军队等）
山峰；底座；乘骑用马；攀，登；运载工具；底座
信封，封皮；包膜；[天] 包层；包迹

etymology.story: "a wrapper, an enclosing cover," specifically a prepared wrapper for a letter or other paper, 1705, from French enveloppe (13c.), a back-formation from envelopper "to envelop" (see envelop)..

1610s, from Middle French acquiescer (16c.), from Latin acquiescere "to become quiet, remain at rest," thus "be satisfied with," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + quiescere "to become quiet," from quies (genitive quietis) "rest, quiet" (see quiet (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 滨水路， 滨水区， 码头区

etymology.story: also water-front, 1834, American English, from water (n.1) + front (n.).
1620s, from Latin depositum, from deponere (see deposit (v.)).

early 15c., from Latin dissipatus, past participle of dissipare "to spread abroad, scatter, disperse; squander, disintegrate," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + supare "to throw, scatter," from PIE *swep- "to throw, sling, cast" (source also of Lithuanian supu "to swing, rock," Old Church Slavonic supo "to strew").

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 破裂 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., of material things, especially dead bodies, also of the soul, morals, etc., from Latin corruptionem (nominative corruptio), noun of action from past participle stem of corrumpere (see corrupt).
1510s, "to enclose with a fence;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 波动， 起伏； 动摇

morphology.mnemonic: 流动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French fluctuacion (12c.) or directly from Latin fluctuationem (nominative fluctuatio) "a wavering, vacillation," noun of action from past participle stem of fluctuare "to undulate, to move in waves," from fluctus "a wave, billow, surge, a flowing," from past participle of fluere "to flow" (see fluent)..

morphology.mnemonic: 相似/相同 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1650s, from Medieval Latin simultaneus, perhaps from simultim "at the same time," extended from Latin simul "at the same time" (see similar (adj.)), or from simul with ending abstracted from Late Latin spontaneus, where the -t- is organic.

late 15c., "act of plundering or pillaging," from Latin praedationem (nominative praedatio) "a plundering, act of taking booty," from praedari "to rob, to plunder," from praeda "plunder, booty, prey" (see prey (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 推荐， 介绍；劝告， 建议

etymology.story: late 14c., "praise, present as worthy," from Medieval Latin recommendare, from Latin re-, here probably an intensive prefix, or else from a sense now obscure (see re-), + commendare "commit to one's care, commend" (see commend).

meanings.definitionCn: 贵族；贵族统治；上层社会；贵族政治

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French aristocracie (Modern French aristocratie), from Late Latin aristocratia, from Greek aristokratia "government or rule of the best," from aristos "best" (originally "most fitting," from PIE *ar-isto-, superlative form of *ar- "to fit together;" see arm (n.1)) + kratos "rule, power" (see -cracy).

late 14c., "one who has held an office or position before the present holder," from Old French predecesseor "forebear" and directly from Late Latin praedecessorem (nominative praedecessor), from Latin prae "before" (see pre-) + decessor "retiring official," from decess-, past participle stem of decedere "go away," also "die" (see decease (n.)).
Old English leccan "to moisten, water, wet, irrigate" (see leak (v.)).
mid-15c., acqueren, from Old French aquerre "acquire, gain, earn, procure," from Vulgar Latin *acquaerere, corresponding to Latin acquirere "to seek in addition to" (see acquisition).

early 15c., nebule "a cloud, mist," from Latin nebula "mist, vapor, fog, smoke, exhalation," figuratively "darkness, obscurity," from PIE *nebh- "cloud" (source also of Sanskrit nabhas- "vapor, cloud, mists, fog, sky;" Greek nephele, nephos "cloud;" German Nebel "fog;" Old English nifol "dark, gloomy;" Welsh niwl "cloud, fog;" Slavic nebo).
1766, from Modern Latin tactica, from Greek taktike (tekhne) "(art of) arrangement," from fem.
"self-propelled motor vehicle," 1895, from French automobile, short for véhicule automobile (see automobile (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 唯物主义的； 物质享乐主义的， 贪图享乐的

etymology.story: 1829, from materialist + -ic.

1861, from French hélicoptère "device for enabling airplanes to rise perpendicularly," thus "flying machine propelled by screws." From Greek helix (genitive helikos) "spiral" (see helix) + pteron "wing" (see ptero-).

meanings.definitionCn: 红利，股息；回报，效益；被除数

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + end

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French dividende "a number divided by another," from Latin dividendum "thing to be divided," neuter gerundive of dividere (see divide).
late 14c., "imprisoned, enslaved," from Latin captivus "caught, taken prisoner," from captus, past participle of capere "to take, hold, seize" (see capable).
"quality ascribed to someone," late 14c., from Latin attributum "anything attributed," noun use of neuter of attributus (see attribute (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 机制；原理，途径；进程；机械装置；技巧

morphology.mnemonic: 机械 + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1660s, from Modern Latin mechanismus, from Greek mekhane "machine, instrument" (see machine (n.))..

late 15c., "make slow or slower," from French retarder "restrain, hold (someone) back, keep (someone from doing something); come to a stop" (13c.) or directly from Latin retardare "make slow, delay, keep back, hinder" (see retardation).
mid-15c., from Old French triumpher (13c.), from Latin triumphare, from triumphus (see triumph (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 察觉， 发觉； 侦查， 探测

etymology.story: early 15c., "exposure, accusation," from Latin detectionem (nominative detectio) "an uncovering," noun of action from past participle stem of detegere (see detect)..
(also steem, extyme), mid-14c., "account, value, worth," from French estime, from estimer (see esteem (v.)).
mid-15c., from Middle French ratification (14c.) and directly from Medieval Latin ratificationem (nominative ratificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of ratificare (see ratify)..

late 15c., "any atmospheric phenomenon," from Middle French meteore (13c.) and directly from Medieval Latin meteorum (nominative meteora), from Greek ta meteora "the celestial phenomena, things in heaven above," plural of meteoron, literally "thing high up," noun use of neuter of meteoros (adj.) "high up, raised from the ground, hanging," from meta- "over, beyond" (see meta-) + -aoros "lifted, hovering in air," related to aeirein "to raise" (see aorta).

1879, from Latin optimum, neuter singular of optimus "best" (used as a superlative of bonus "good"), probably related to ops "power, resources" (in which case the evolution is from "richest" to "the most esteemed," thus from PIE root *op- "to work") or to ob "in front of," with superlative suffix *-tumos.

meanings.definitionCn: 重力的, 引力作用的

morphology.mnemonic: be affected by gravity + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1816, from gravitation + -al (1).

Old English bur "room, hut, dwelling, chamber," from Proto-Germanic *buraz (source also of Old Norse bur "chamber," Swedish bur "cage," Old High German bur "dwelling, chamber," German Vogelbauer "(bird)cage"), from *bu- "to dwell," from PIE root *bheue- "to be, exist, dwell" (see be).

meanings.definitionCn: 说服，劝说；使某人相信；劝某人做（不做）某事

etymology.story: 1510s, from Middle French persuader (14c.), from Latin persuadere "to bring over by talking," (see persuasion).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治/首领 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

meanings.definitionCn: (意见等的)一致，一致同意，共识

etymology.story: 1854 as a term in physiology; 1861 of persons; from Latin consensus "agreement, accord," past participle of consentire (see consent (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 战争 + 有点…/像…

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to render beautiful," from Old French embelliss-, stem of embellir "make beautiful, ornament," from assimilated form of en- (see en- (1)) + bel "beautiful," from Latin bellus "handsome, pretty, fine" (see bene-).
"science of versification," 1760, from Greek he metrike "prosody," plural of metron "meter, a verse; that by which anything is measured; measure, length, size, limit, proportion" (see meter (n.2))..
1550s, "one who engages in mental speculation," from Latin speculator "a looker-out, spy, scout, explorer; investigator, examiner," agent noun from speculari (see speculation).

1520s, from Late Latin dilemma, from Greek dilemma "double proposition," a technical term in rhetoric, from di- "two" + lemma "premise, anything received or taken," from root of lambanein "to take" (see analemma).

meanings.definitionCn: 小地毯；毛皮地毯；男子假发

etymology.story: 1550s, "coarse fabric," of Scandinavian origin; compare Norwegian dialectal rugga "coarse coverlet," from Old Norse rogg "shaggy tuft," from Proto-Germanic *rawwa-, perhaps related to rag (n.) and rough (adj.).

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 做/驱动

etymology.story: early 15c., "act the part of, represent in performance," from en- (1) "make, put in" + act (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + body体（corpor也作corp） + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: late 14c., "to put (something) into the body or substance of (something else), blend; absorb, eat," also "solidify, harden," often in medical writing, from Late Latin incorporatus, past participle of incorporare "unite into one body, embody, include," from Latin in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + verb from corpus (genitive corporis) "body" (see corporeal).
"black lead," 1796, from German Graphit, coined 1789 by German mineralogist Abraham Gottlob Werner (1750-1817) from Greek graphein "write" (see -graphy) + mineral suffix -ite.
1670s, from scrutiny + -ize.
early 14c., "a quarrel, dispute, disagreement," from Old French debat; see debate (v.).
also black-out, 1908 in the theatrical sense of a darkened stage, from black + out.

1540s, from Latin extinguere/exstinguere "quench, put out (what is burning), wipe out, obliterate," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + stinguere "quench," apparently an evolved sense from PIE *steig- "to prick, stick, pierce" (see stick (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 易受攻击的，易受…的攻击；易受伤害的；有弱点的
友好关系；加入；联盟；从属关系

etymology.story: 1751, "adoption," from French affiliation, from Medieval Latin affiliationem (nominative affiliatio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin affiliare "to adopt a son," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + filius "son" (see filial).

meanings.definitionCn: 通货膨胀的；通货膨胀倾向的；通货膨胀引起的

morphology.mnemonic: in + 吹/膨胀 + 状态/行为/过程 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1916, from inflation + -ary..
1819, back-formation from donation.

meanings.definitionCn: 倒空； 枯竭； 消耗

etymology.story: 1807, back-formation from depletion.

meanings.definitionCn: 至高无上的；有主权的；拥有最高统治权的；不折不扣的；极好的
君主；独立国；最高统治者

etymology.story: early 14c., "great, superior, supreme," from Old French soverain "highest, supreme, chief," from Vulgar Latin *superanus "chief, principal" (source also of Spanish soberano, Italian soprano), from Latin super "over" (see super-).
1852, "one numerical statistic," see statistics.

meanings.definitionCn: 禁忌， 忌讳；禁止 A

etymology.story: also tabu, 1777 (in Cook's "A Voyage to the Pacific Ocean"), "consecrated, inviolable, forbidden, unclean or cursed," explained in some English sources as being from Tongan (Polynesian language of the island of Tonga) ta-bu "sacred," from ta "mark" + bu "especially." But this may be folk etymology, as linguists in the Pacific have reconstructed an irreducable Proto-Polynesian *tapu, from Proto-Oceanic *tabu "sacred, forbidden" (compare Hawaiian kapu "taboo, prohibition, sacred, holy, consecrated;" Tahitian tapu "restriction, sacred;" Maori tapu "be under ritual restriction, prohibited").
1540s, "noble, magnificent" (of buildings, etc.), from Latin superbus "grand, proud, splendid; haughty, vain, insolent," from super "above, over" (see super-).

meanings.definitionCn: 浇铸， 造型； 塑造， 形成
有意义的;重大的,重要的

morphology.mnemonic: 标记 + 使…化/造成 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, "having a meaning," from Latin significantem (nominative significans, present participle of significare "make known, indicate" (see signify).

meanings.definitionCn: 大胆的，英勇的；黑体的；厚颜无耻的；险峻的

etymology.story: Old English beald (West Saxon), bald (Anglian) "bold, brave, confident, strong," from Proto-Germanic *balthaz (source also of Old High German bald "bold, swift," in names such as Archibald, Leopold, Theobald; Gothic balþei "boldness;" Old Norse ballr "frightful, dangerous"), perhaps from PIE *bhol-to- suffixed form of *bhel- (2) "to blow, swell" (see bole).

morphology.mnemonic: du + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1620s, "to double," from Latin duplicatus, past participle of duplicare (see duplicate (adj.)).
1590s, "to make a catalogue;" see catalogue (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 放纵的， 不受约束的

etymology.story: late 14c., originally in figurative sense of "unrestrained, ungoverned," from un- (1) "not" + bridled (see bridle (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 部分重叠；部分的同时发生

etymology.story: 1560s, from de- "down" + base (adj.) "low," on analogy of abase (or, alternatively, from obsolete verb base "to abuse")..

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 射线/放射 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "destroy utterly," literally "pull up by the roots," from Latin eradicatus, past participle of eradicare "to root out, annihilate" (see eradication).

morphology.mnemonic: 父亲 + ot + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1726, from patriot + -ism..

筛选；囫囵吞下；（把门、窗等）闩上；突然说出，脱口说出
突然地；像箭似地；直立地
移动；随季节而移居；移往

morphology.mnemonic: 移动/迁移 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1690s, from Latin migratus, past participle of migrare "to move from one place to another" (see migration).

"mutually agreed-upon temporary intermission of hostilities," early 13c., triws, variant of trewes, originally plural of trewe "faith, assurance of faith, covenant, treaty," from Old English treow "faith, truth, fidelity; pledge, promise, agreement, treaty," from Proto-Germanic *treuwaz- (source also of Old Frisian triuwe, Middle Dutch trouwe, Dutch trouw, Old High German triuwa, German treue, Gothic triggwa "faith, faithfulness").

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 步/走 + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1690s, from Latin congressionem (from congressus, see congress) + -al (1).

Old English streng "line, cord, thread, string of a bow or harp," in plural "tackle, rigging; lineage, race," from Proto-Germanic *strangiz (source also of Old Norse strengr, Danish streng, Middle Dutch strenge, Dutch streng, Old High German strang, German Strang "rope, cord"), from *strang- "taut, stiff," from PIE root *strenk- "tight, narrow." Gradually restricted by early Middle English to lines that are smaller than a rope.

late 14c., "to place (oneself) under the control of another, to yield oneself," from Latin submittere "to yield, lower, let down, put under, reduce," from sub "under" (see sub-) + mittere "let go, send" (see mission).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 流动 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., "flowing," from Middle French affluent (14c.) or directly from Latin affluentem (nominative affluens) "flowing toward, abounding, rich, copious," present participle of affluere "flow toward," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + fluere "to flow" (see fluent)..
mid-15c., earliest use was for bees and wasps, probably echoic of humming.
"hair of a man's face" (usually plural), c.

mid-15c., from Middle French aboliss-, present participle stem of abolir "to abolish" (15c.), from Latin abolere "destroy, cause to die out, retard the growth of," perhaps from ab- "from" (see ab-) + adolere "to grow," from PIE *ol-eye-, causative of root *al- (3) "to grow, nourish" (see old), and perhaps formed as an antonym to adolere.

mid-13c., faith, feith, fei, fai "faithfulness to a trust or promise; loyalty to a person; honesty, truthfulness," from Anglo-French and Old French feid, foi "faith, belief, trust, confidence; pledge" (11c.), from Latin fides "trust, faith, confidence, reliance, credence, belief," from root of fidere "to trust," from PIE root *bheidh- "to trust" (source also of Greek pistis "faith, confidence, honesty;" see bid).

late 14c., from Old French respirer (12c.), from Latin respirare "breathe again, breathe in and out," from re- "again" (see re-) + spirare "to breathe" (see spirit (n.)).

late 14c., jerarchie, ierarchie, "rank in the sacred order; one of the three divisions of the nine orders of angels;" loosely, "rule, dominion," from Old French ierarchie (14c., Modern French hiérarchie), from Medieval Latin hierarchia "ranked division of angels" (in the system of Dionysius the Areopagite), from Greek hierarkhia "rule of a high priest," from hierarkhes "high priest, leader of sacred rites," from ta hiera "the sacred rites" (neuter plural of hieros "sacred;" see ire) + arkhein "to lead, rule" (see archon).

1740, from pay (v.) + roll (n.); "total amount paid to employees over a period," hence, via records-keeping, "list of employees receiving pay.".

1610s, Latin, literally "former," comparative of ante "before" (see ante).

1540s, originally mostly in civic crown (Latin corona civica), a chaplet of oak leaves awarded to one who saved the life of a fellow citizen in battle, from Latin civicus "of a citizen," adjectival derivation of civis "townsman" (see city).
1823, from French viabilité, from viable (see viable)..
1727, from refine + -ery.

"to set or place apart, to detach so as to make alone," by 1786, a back-formation from isolated (q.v.).

etymology.story: also multi-national, by 1921, from multi- + national.

meanings.definitionCn: 灭绝， 绝种； 熄灭

etymology.story: early 15c., "annihilation," from Latin extinctionem/exstinctionem (nominative extinctio/exstinctio) "extinction, annihilation," noun of action from past participle stem of extinguere/exstinguere "quench, wipe out" (see extinguish).

late 15c., from Middle French metaphore (Old French metafore, 13c.), and directly from Latin metaphora, from Greek metaphora "a transfer," especially of the sense of one word to a different word, literally "a carrying over," from metapherein "transfer, carry over; change, alter; to use a word in a strange sense," from meta- "over, across" (see meta-) + pherein "to carry, bear" (see infer)..

"following in time, later," mid-15c., from Middle French subsequent (14c.) and directly from Latin subsequentem (nominative subsequens), present participle of subsequi "come after in time, follow closely," figuratively "imitate, conform to," from sub "closely, up to" (see sub-) + sequi "follow" (see suit (n.)).

mid-15c., "desert, abandon;" late 15c., "give up, desist," from Old French relinquiss-, present participle stem of relinquir (12c.), from Latin relinquere "leave behind, forsake, abandon, give up," from re- "back" (see re-) + linquere "to leave," from PIE *linkw-, from root *leikw- "to leave behind" (source also of Sanskrit reknas "inheritance, wealth," rinakti "leaves;" Greek leipein "to leave;" Gothic leihvan, Old English lænan "to lend;" Old High German lihan "to borrow;" Old Norse lan "loan").

1650s, "to point out," back-formation from indication (q.v.) or else from Latin indicatus, past participle of indicare "to point out, show, indicate." Especially "to give suggestion of, be reason for inferring" (1706).

"render immobile," 1843, from immobile + -ize.
1845 in biological sense, from German metabolisch (1839), from Greek metabolikos "changeable," from metabole "a change, changing, a transition" (see metabolism).

1550s (earlier in Latin form piramis, late 14c.), from French pyramide (Old French piramide "obelisk, stela," 12c.), from Latin pyramides, plural of pyramis "one of the pyramids of Egypt," from Greek pyramis (plural pyramides) "a pyramid," apparently an alteration of Egyptian pimar "pyramid." 1640s, "to be equivalent;" 1650s, "to counterbalance, make up for," from Latin compensatus, past participle of compensare "to weigh one thing (against another)," thus, "to counterbalance," from com- "with" (see com-) + pensare, frequentative of pendere "to weigh" (see pendant).

meanings.definitionCn: 办理,处理;交易,事务;(pl.)会报,学报

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 做/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "the adjustment of a dispute, a negotiated agreement, management or settlement of an affair," from Old French transaccion "exchange, transaction," from Late Latin transactionem (nominative transactio) "an agreement, accomplishment," noun of action from past participle stem of transigere "stab through; accomplish, perform, drive or carry through, come to a settlement," from trans- "through" (see trans-) + agere "to drive" (see act (n.)).
1905, blend of smoke and fog, formed "after Lewis Carrol's example" [Klein; see portmanteau].

meanings.definitionCn: 证明，证实；发证书(或执照)给

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to declare the truth of," also "to vouch for or confirm" (an official record, etc.), from Old French certefiier "make certain, witness the truth of" (12c.), from Late Latin certificare "to certify, to make certain," from Latin certus (see certain) + root of facere "to make, do" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 凌辱，强奸；对…施暴行；激起愤怒

etymology.story: 1620s, from French coloration (16c.), from Late Latin colorationem (nominative coloratio) "act or fact of coloring," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin colorare "to color, to get tanned," from color (see color (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: (好)孤独的；偏僻的；单一的，唯一的

morphology.mnemonic: 太阳/独自 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: mid-14c., "alone, living alone," from Old French solitaire, from Latin solitarius "alone, lonely, isolated," from solitas "loneliness, solitude," from solus "alone" (see sole (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 攀登；使混杂，仓促凑成；扰乱
抢夺，争夺；混乱，混乱的一团；爬行，攀登

morphology.mnemonic: that which is common, + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1811 in reference to communes; 1843 in reference to communities, from French communal (Old French comunal, 12c.), from Late Latin communalis, from communa (see commune (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 有营养的， 营养丰富的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Latin nutricius "that which nourishes, nurses," from nutrix (genitive nutricis) "a nurse," from nutrire (see nourish).

morphology.mnemonic: 狂热 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "the coming to a head of a boil, etc.; a state of producing pus," from Middle French maturation and directly from Latin maturationem (nominative maturatio), noun of action from past participle stem of maturare "to ripen, make ripe" (see mature (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 使锐利；将…开刃；给…加上边
分离；使分离；劈开；离开；分解

etymology.story: "results, profits," 1660s, from proceed (v.) on the notion of "that which proceeds" from some event or activity..

morphology.mnemonic: in + 放置 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "a tax, duty, tribute," from Old French imposicion "tax, duty; a fixing" (early 14c.), from Latin impositionem (nominative impositio) "a laying on," noun of action from past participle stem of imponere "to place upon," from assimilated form of in- "into, in" (see in- (2)) + ponere "to put, place" (past participle positus; see position (n.)).
1590s, from relent + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 要求； 主张， 声称；索赔
要求， 主张； 断言；索赔；权利， 要求权， 所有权

etymology.story: early 14c., "a demand of a right; right of claiming," from Old French claime "claim, complaint," from clamer (see claim (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 古老的，年代久远的；过时的，古董的；古风的，古式的
古董，古玩；古风，古希腊和古罗马艺术风格
慢性的；长期的；习惯性的

etymology.story: early 15c., of diseases, "lasting a long time," from Middle French chronique, from Latin chronicus, from Greek khronikos "of time, concerning time," from khronos "time" (see chrono-).

meanings.definitionCn: 生手， 新手； 初学者； 新信徒， 初学修士

etymology.story: mid-14c., "probationer in a religious order," from Old French novice "beginner" (12c.), from Medieval Latin novicius, noun use of Latin novicius "newly imported, newly arrived, inexperienced" (of slaves), from novus "new" (see new).

1660s, from French toxique and directly from Late Latin toxicus "poisoned," from Latin toxicum "poison," from Greek toxikon (pharmakon) "(poison) for use on arrows," from toxikon, neuter of toxikos "pertaining to arrows or archery," and thus to a bow, from toxon "bow," probably from a Scythian word that also was borrowed into Latin as taxus "yew." Watkins suggests a possible source in Iranian taxša- "bow," from PIE *tekw- "to run, flee." As a noun from 1890..

meanings.definitionCn: 判断力； 谨慎， 审慎； 明智

morphology.mnemonic: separate, distinct + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 保护区；禁猎地；加工成的食品

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 保持/服务

etymology.story: 1590s, from re- "back, again" + kindle (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 威慑， 吓住， 断念

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin deterrere "to frighten from, discourage from," from de- "away" (see de-) + terrere "frighten" (see terrible).

meanings.definitionCn: 进入；入口；条目；登记；报关手续；对土地的侵占

etymology.story: 1690s, as a term in logic, "deductive," from French synthétique (17c.) and directly from Modern Latin syntheticus, from Greek synthetikos "skilled in putting together, constructive," from synthetos "put together, constructed, compounded," past participle of syntithenai "to put together" (see synthesis).
"residence of a military commander," 1640s, from head (adj.) + quarters.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 引导 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of deducting," from Middle French déduction or directly from Latin deductionem (nominative deductio), noun of action from past participle stem of deducere (see deduce).

1560s, "pertaining to public revenue," from Middle French fiscal, from Late Latin fiscalis "of or belonging to the state treasury," from Latin fiscus "state treasury," originally "money bag, purse, basket made of twigs (in which money was kept)," which is of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 宣告，公布；声明；表明；赞扬

etymology.story: late 14c., proclamen, from Latin proclamare "cry or call out," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + clamare "to cry out" (see claim (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 保持/服务 + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1835, in reference to the Conservative party in British politics; from conservative + -ism.
early 15c., "line around a figure or surface," from Latin perimetros, from Greek perimetron "circumference," from peri-  "around" (see peri-) + metron "measure" (see meter (n.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 预言， 预料， 预报

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 说/讲 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French prédiction and directly from Medieval Latin predictionem (nominative predictio), from Latin praedictio "a foretelling," noun of action from past participle stem of praedicere (see predict)..

early 15c., retreve, originally in reference to dogs finding lost game, from Middle French retruev-, stem of Old French retreuver (Modern French retrouver) "find again, recover, meet again, recognize," from re- "again" (see re-) + trouver "to find," probably from Vulgar Latin *tropare "to compose" (see trove).

meanings.definitionCn: 有礼貌的，高雅的；端正的

etymology.story: 1660s, from Latin decorus "becoming, seemly, fitting, proper," from decus (genitive decoris) "ornament" (see decorate).

meanings.definitionCn: 取代；置换；转移；把…免职；排水

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French desplacer (15c.), from des- (see dis-) + placer "to place."

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + domin + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, from Middle French prédominant (14c.), from Medieval Latin *praedominantem (nominative praedominans), present participle of *praedominare, from Latin prae- "before" (see pre-) + dominari "to rule" (see dominate).

meanings.definitionCn: 驯养， 教化； 使喜爱家居生活

etymology.story: 1630s, of animals; 1741, of persons, "to cause to be attached to home and family;" from Medieval Latin domesticatus, past participle of domesticare "to tame," literally "to dwell in a house," from domesticus (see domestic).

meanings.definitionCn: 意识形态，(政治或社会的)思想意识

morphology.mnemonic: 思想/观念 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1796, "science of ideas," originally "philosophy of the mind which derives knowledge from the senses" (as opposed to metaphysics), from French idéologie "study or science of ideas," coined by French philosopher Destutt de Tracy (1754-1836) from idéo- "of ideas," from Greek idea (see idea) + -logie (see -logy).

meanings.definitionCn: 产生相反效果的， 适得其反的

etymology.story: also counter-productive, counter productive, 1920, American English, from counter- + productive..

1580s, "medical prescription," from Middle French récipé (15c.), from Latin recipe "take!," second person imperative singular of recipere "to take" (see receive); word written by physicians at the head of prescriptions.
1570s, from Middle French apportionner, from Old French aporcioner "apportion, share out," from a- "to" (see ad-) + portioner "to divide into portions," from portion "share, portion" (see portion).

meanings.definitionCn: 敌意，敌对，对抗；抵制，反对，否决；

etymology.story: early 15c., "hostile action," from Middle French hostilité "enmity" (15c.), or directly from Late Latin hostilitatem (nominative hostilitas) "enmity," from Latin hostilis "inimical," from hostis "enemy" (see guest (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 自我/自动 + 生命/生物 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1797, from auto- + biography.

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: veri + thing or animal spoken about before. + able

etymology.story: early 15c., from Anglo-French and Old French veritable "true, real, truthful, valid (in law)," from verité (see verity) + -able.

1828, in part a back-formation from pseudonymous, in part from German pseudonym and French pseudonyme (adj.), from Greek pseudonymos "having a false name, under a false name," from pseudes "false" (see pseudo-) + onyma, Aeolic dialectal variant of onoma "name" (see name (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [生化] 血红蛋白（等于haemoglobin）；血红素

etymology.story: also hæmoglobin, coloring matter in red blood cells, 1862, shortening of hæmatoglobin (1845), from Greek haimato-, comb.

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 保持/服务 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., preservacioun "protection from disease," from Old French preservacion (13c.), from Medieval Latin preservationem (nominative preservatio), noun of action from past participle stem of preservare (see preserve (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 相当大(或多)的,可观的;值得考虑的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "capable of being considered," from Medieval Latin considerabilis "worthy to be considered," from Latin considerare (see consider).
"organic poison," especially one produced by bacteria in an animal body, 1886, from toxic + -in (2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 储备，储存；自然保护区；预备队；缄默；[金融] 储备金

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 转 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "having naturally no backbone," 1819, from Latin in- "not" (see in- (1)) + vertebratus (Pliny), from vertebra "joint or articulation of the body, joint of the spine" (see vertebra).

meanings.definitionCn: 可行的， 可实施的； 【生】 能自行生长发育的

etymology.story: 1828, from French viable "capable of life" (1530s), from vie "life" (from Latin vita "life;" see vital) + -able.

root.meaning: to cover with something in front

morphology.mnemonic: to cover with something in front + 与…有关的/形容词

meanings.definitionCn: 收回;(from)恢复,痊愈;重新获得

etymology.story: 1620s, from French influx (16c.) or directly from Late Latin influxus "a flowing in," from past participle stem of Latin influere "to flow in" (see influence (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 错误地/不当地 + 心脏 + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: 1580s, "mistake, error;" 1610s, "misbehavior;" see miscarry + -age.
also clock-wise, "in the direction of the hands of a clock," 1879, from clock (n.1) + wise (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 破坏， 毁坏； 使震惊， 使极为悲痛

etymology.story: 1630s, perhaps a back-formation from devastation.

meanings.definitionCn: (使)上船(或飞机,汽车等);着手,从事

etymology.story: 1540s (transitive); 1570s (intransitive), from Middle French embarquer, from assimilated form of en- "in" (see en- (1)) + barque "small ship" (see bark (n.)).
1833 (Lyell) in the geological sense, in reference to rock whose form has been changed by heat or pressure, from metamorphosis + -ic.

meanings.definitionCn: 奇迹，奇事；奇才；奇观；预兆

etymology.story: late 15c., "sign, portent, something extraordinary from which omens are drawn," from Latin prodigium "prophetic sign, omen, portent, prodigy," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + -igium, a suffix or word of unknown origin, perhaps from *agi-, root of aio "I say" (see adage).

meanings.definitionCn: 附带条件；附文；限制性条款

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Medieval Latin proviso (quod) "provided (that)," phrase at the beginning of clauses in legal documents (mid-14c.), from Latin proviso "it being provided," ablative neuter of provisus, past participle of providere (see provide).

morphology.mnemonic: medic + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "medical treatment of a disease or wound," from Middle French médication and directly from Latin medicationem (nominative medicatio) "healing, cure," from past participle stem of medicare, medicari "to medicate, heal, cure" (poetic and Late Latin) from medicus "physician, healing" (see medical (adj.))..

mid-15c., "body of a statute," from Anglo-French purveuest "it is provided," or purveu que "provided that" (late 13c.), clauses that introduced statutes in old legal documents, from Anglo-French purveu, Old French porveu (Modern French pourvu) "provided," past participle of porveoir "to provide," from Latin providere "make ready" (see provide).

1560s, "a chafing, rubbing," from Middle French friction (16c.) and directly from Latin frictionem (nominative frictio) "a rubbing, rubbing down," noun of action from past participle stem of fricare "to rub, rub down," which is of uncertain origin.

1530s, from Middle French conférer (14c.) "to give, converse, compare," from Latin conferre "to bring together," figuratively "to compare; consult, deliberate, talk over," from com- "together" (see com-) + ferre "to bear" (see infer).
1520s, from Middle French laxité, from Latin laxitatem (nominative laxitas) "width, spaciousness," from laxus "loose, lax" (see lax).

meanings.definitionCn: 大厅；休息室；会客室；游说议员的团体
承认；答谢；报偿；告知已收到

etymology.story: 1550s, a blend of Middle English aknow (from Old English oncnawan "understand," from on + cnawan "recognize;" see know) and Middle English knowlechen "admit, acknowledge" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 休眠的； 静止的； 隐匿的

etymology.story: late 14c., "fixed in place," from Old French dormant (12c.), present participle of dormir "to sleep," from Latin dormire "to sleep," from PIE root *drem- "to sleep" (source also of Old Church Slavonic dremati "to sleep, doze," Greek edrathon "I slept," Sanskrit drati "sleeps").
mid-15c., from Old French receptour or directly from Latin receptor, agent noun from recipere (see receive).

1520s, from Middle French équiper "to fit out," from Old French esquiper "fit out a ship, load on board" (12c.), probably from Old Norse skipa "arrange, place in order," usually "fit out a ship," but also of warriors manning a hall and trees laden with ripe fruit, from skip "ship" (see ship (n.)).
"document or book written by hand," 1590s (adj.), c.

morphology.mnemonic: de + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1822, from deferent + -ial; as a word in anatomy, from 1877.

meanings.definitionCn: 遗传的， 遗传性的； 可继承的， 世袭的

etymology.story: early 15c., "transmitted in a line of progeny," hereditarie, from Latin hereditarius "inherited; of or relating to an inheritance," from hereditas "heirship, inheritance" (see heredity).

morphology.mnemonic: dia + gon + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1540s (implied in diagonally), from Middle French diagonal, from Latin diagonalis, from diagonus "slanting line," from Greek diagonios "from angle to angle," from dia- "across" (see dia-) + gonia "angle" (see -gon).

meanings.definitionCn: 在内地；向内地；向内陆；在内陆

etymology.story: "of or pertaining to interior parts of a country," 1550s, from in + land (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 姿势； 观点， 立场

etymology.story: 1530s, "standing place, station," probably from Middle French stance "resting place, harbor" (16c.), from Vulgar Latin *stantia "place, abode" (also source of Italian stanza "stopping place, station, stanza," Spanish stancia "a dwelling"), from Latin stans (genitive stantis), present participle of stare "to stand," from PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

morphology.mnemonic: in + fringe + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: "a break or breach" (of a contract, right, etc.), from infringe + -ment.
1799, from iridescent + -ence.

"pertaining to strategy, characterized by strategy," 1807, from French stratégique and directly from Greek strategikos in classical use "of or for a general; fitted for command," from strategos (see strategy).

meanings.definitionCn: 容易地；乐意地；无困难地
一致的，协调的；(话语等)条理清楚的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + _here + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French cohérent (16c.), from Latin cohaerentem (nominative cohaerens), present participle of cohaerere "cohere," from com- "together" (see co-)  + haerere "to stick" (see hesitation)..

meanings.definitionCn: 秘密的，私下的；偷偷摸摸的

etymology.story: 1560s, from Latin clandestinus "secret, hidden," from clam "secretly," from adverbial derivative of base of celare "to hide" (see cell), perhaps on model of intestinus "internal."

meanings.definitionCn: 三角洲； 希腊字母表的第四个字母

etymology.story: "place of residence," mid-14c., verbal noun from dwell (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 适应； 改编； 改制物

root.meaning: to undergo modification so as to fit new circumstances

morphology.mnemonic: to undergo modification so as to fit new circumstances + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "agreement, conformity," from Latin convenientia "meeting together, agreement, harmony," from conveniens, present participle of convenire (see convene).

mid-14c., "decision, sentence," from Old French déterminacion (14c.) "determination, settlement, definition," from Latin determinationem (nominative determinatio) "conclusion, boundary," noun of action from past participle stem of determinare "to enclose, bound, set limits to" (see determine).

meanings.definitionCn: 插曲；一段情节；插话；有趣的事件

etymology.story: 1670s, "commentary between two choric songs in a Greek tragedy," also "an incidental narrative or digression within a story, poem, etc.," from French épisode or directly from Greek epeisodion "an episode," literally "an addition," noun use of neuter of epeisodios "coming in besides," from epi "in addition" (see epi-) + eisodos "a coming in, entrance" (from eis "into" + hodos "way").
1660s, from Latin calculus "reckoning, account," originally "pebble used as a reckoning counter," diminutive of calx (genitive calcis) "limestone" (see chalk (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 脚/儿童 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 14c., "advantageous, fit, proper to a purpose," from Old French expedient "useful, beneficial" (14c.) or directly from Latin expedientem (nominative expediens) "beneficial," present participle of expedire "make fit or ready, prepare" (see expedite).

meanings.definitionCn: 赋予生命； 使生机勃勃

morphology.mnemonic: 精神/心灵 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "alive," late 14c., from Latin animatus (see animate (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 对/向 + 投/掷 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., "something sent forth," from Middle French émission (14c.) and directly from Latin emissionem (nominative emissio) "a sending out, a projecting, hurling, letting go, releasing," noun of action from past participle stem of emittere "send out" (see emit).

1660s, "thrust forth, push out," from Latin exertus/exsertus, past participle of exerere/exserere "thrust out, put forth," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + serere "attach, join; arrange, line up" (see series).

"situated in the rear, pertaining to the rear, toward the back," late 14c., probably from an unrecorded Old English adjective from hinder (adv.) "behind, back, afterward," but treated as a comparative of hind (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 气味；嗅觉；痕迹；察觉能力
闻到；发觉；使充满…的气味；循着遗臭追踪

etymology.story: 1530s, "appraise the worth of," from Latin aestimatus, past participle of aestimare "to value, appraise" (see esteem (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 毕业证书，学位证书；公文，文书；奖状

etymology.story: 1640s, "state paper, official document," from Latin diploma, from Greek diploma "license, chart," originally "paper folded double," from diploun "to double, fold over," from diploos "double" (see diploid) + -oma.
also pin-point, "point of a pin," 1849, from pin (n.) + point (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 不充分的； 不适当的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 统治 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1670s; see in- (1) "not, opposite of" + adequate.

meanings.definitionCn: 不可避免的,必然发生的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + age年龄，寿命，时代，时期

etymology.story: "unavoidable," mid-15c., from Latin inevitabilis "unavoidable," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + evitabilis "avoidable," from evitare "to avoid," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + vitare "shun," originally "go out of the way." As a noun from 1850.

meanings.definitionCn: 不现实的， 不切实际的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + real + …主义者/…专家 + ic

etymology.story: 1865, from un- (1) "not" + realistic.

late 14c., "track cut through a wood," later "long, narrow ditch" (late 15c.), from Old French trenche "a slice, cut, gash, slash; defensive ditch" (13c., Modern French tranche), from trenchier "to cut, carve, slice," possibly from Vulgar Latin *trincare, from Latin truncare "to cut or lop off" (see truncate).

late 14c., "refreshment or curing of a person, refreshment by eating," from Old French recreacion (13c.), from Latin recreationem (nominative recreatio) "recovery from illness," noun of action from past participle stem of recreare "to refresh, restore, make anew, revive, invigorate," from re- "again" (see re-) + creare (see create).

meanings.definitionCn: 补贴，津贴；零用钱；减价，折扣；允许

etymology.story: late 14c., "praise" (a sense now obsolete), from Old French aloance "allowance, granting, allocation," from alouer (see allow).

Meaning extended to "sexism" via male chauvinism (1969).
1867; see dose + -age, perhaps on model of French dosage (1812)..

Old English delfan "to dig" (class III strong verb; past tense dealf, past participle dolfen), common West Germanic verb (cognates: Old Saxon delban, Dutch delven, Middle High German telben "to dig"), from PIE root *dhelbh- (source also of Lithuanian delba "crowbar," Russian dolbit', Czech dlabati, Polish dłubać "to chisel;" Russian dolotó, Czech dlato, Polish dłuto "chisel").

morphology.mnemonic: 死亡 + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

late 14c., "to decide, resolve; to arrange the time of (a meeting, etc.)," from Anglo-French appointer, Old French apointier "make ready, arrange, settle, place" (12c.), from apointer "duly, fitly," from phrase à point "to the point," from a- "to" (see ad-) + point "point," from Latin punctum (see point (n.)).

1660s (trans.), from Latin ignitus, past participle of ignire "set on fire, make red hot," from ignis "fire" (see igneous).

*gen- "to give birth, beget, produce" (see genus).

early 15c., from Old French fraternel "brotherly, fraternal," and directly from Medieval Latin fraternalis, from Latin fraternus "friendly, closely allied," literally "brotherly" (see fraternity).

1620s, from Latin praevius "going before," from prae "before" (see pre-) + via "road" (see via).

1550s, "an index, list, catalogue," from Late Latin repertorium "inventory, list," from Latin repertus, past participle of reperire "to find, get, invent," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + parire, archaic form of paerere "produce, bring forth," from PIE root *pere- (1) "to bring forth" (see pare).
1798, from anthropology + -ist..
late 14c., from cumber (v.) + -some (1).

"matter used in vaccination," 1846, from French vaccin, noun use of adjective, from Latin vaccina, fem.

Old English drugað, drugoð "drought, dryness, desert," from Proto-Germanic *drugothaz, from Germanic root *dreug- "dry" (cf high/height) with *-itho, Germanic suffix for forming abstract nouns (see -th (2)).

Therapeutic was used from 1540s as a noun meaning "the branch of medicine concerned with treatment of disease."
"traditional beliefs and customs of the common people," 1846, coined by antiquarian William J.

Old English timber "building, structure," in late Old English "building material, trees suitable for building," and "trees or woods in general," from Proto-Germanic *timran (source also of Old Saxon timbar "a building, room," Old Frisian timber "wood, building," Old High German zimbar "timber, wooden dwelling, room," Old Norse timbr "timber," German Zimmer "room"), from PIE *deme- "to build," possibly from root *dem- "house, household" (source of Greek domos, Latin domus; see domestic (adj.)).
in early use also inshrine, 1580s, from en- (1) "make, put in" + shrine (n.).
late 14c., "of seed or semen," from Old French seminal (14c.) and directly from Latin seminalis, from semen (genitive seminis) "seed" (see semen).

Meaning "to use a model to imitate certain conditions for purposes of study or training" is from 1947.

"principle, opinion, or dogma maintained as true by a person, sect, school, etc.," properly "a thing held (to be true)," early 15c., from Latin tenet "he holds," third person singular present indicative of tenere "to hold, grasp, keep, have possession, maintain," also "reach, gain, acquire, obtain; hold back, repress, restrain;" figuratively "hold in mind, take in, understand."

The Latin word is from PIE root *ten- "to stretch" (source also of Sanskrit tantram "loom," tanoti "stretches, lasts;" Persian tar "string;" Lithuanian tankus "compact," i.e.

etymology.story: early 15c., "deciding by one's own discretion," from Old French arbitraire (14c.) or directly from Latin arbitrarius "depending on the will, uncertain," from arbiter (see arbiter).

meanings.definitionCn: 致敬；招呼；向欢呼；猛发；使像下雹样落下（过去式hailed，过去分词hailed，现在分词hailing，第三人称单数hails）

etymology.story: "frozen rain, pellets of ice falling in showers," Old English hægl, hagol (Mercian hegel) "hail, hailstorm," also the name of the rune for H, from Proto-Germanic *haglaz (source also of Old Frisian heil, Old Saxon, Old High German hagal, Old Norse hagl, German Hagel "hail"), probably from PIE *kaghlo- "pebble" (source also of Greek kakhlex "round pebble")..

meanings.definitionCn: 专心的， 专注的； 急切的

etymology.story: late 14c., "very attentive, eager," from Latin intentus "attentive, eager, waiting, strained," past participle of intendere "to strain, stretch" (see intend).

meanings.definitionCn: 被动的，消极的；被动语态的

etymology.story: late 14c., in grammatical sense (opposed to active), Old French passif "suffering, undergoing hardship" (14c.) and directly from Latin passivus "capable of feeling or suffering," from pass-, past participle stem of pati "to suffer" (see passion).

meanings.definitionCn: [肥料] 肥料；受精媒介物；促进发展者

etymology.story: 1660s, "something that fertilizes (land)," agent noun from fertilize.
1520s, from Latin efficacia "efficacy, efficiency," from efficax (genitive efficacis) "powerful, effectual, efficient," from stem of efficere "work out, accomplish" (see effect (n.)).

1580s, "trade wind of the Indian Ocean," from Dutch monssoen, from Portuguese monçao, from Arabic mawsim "time of year, appropriate season" (for a voyage, pilgrimage, etc.), from wasama "he marked." When it blows from the southwest (April through October) it brings heavy rain, hence "heavy episode of rainfall during the rainy season" (1747)..

Old English gebecnian (West Saxon beacnian) "to make a mute sign," derivative of beacen "a sign, beacon," from Proto-Germanic *bauknjan (source also of Old Saxon boknian, Old High German bouhnen), from PIE root *bha- (1) "to shine" (see beacon).

meanings.definitionCn: 相当的，相应的；一致的；通信的

etymology.story: 1570s, past participle adjective from correspond.

1640s, from French granit(e) (17c.) or directly from Italian granito "granite," originally "grained," past participle adjective from granire "granulate, make grainy," from grano "grain," from Latin granum "grain" (see grain (n.)).
1560s, devest (modern spelling is c.

late 14c., "treatment, discussion," from Anglo-French treté, Old French traitié "assembly, agreement, dealings," from Latin tractatus "discussion, handling, management," from tractare "to handle, manage" (see treat (v.)).
"to dig up," mid-15c., from un- (2) "opposite of" + earth (v.) "bury (a corpse) in the ground" (c.
1620s, from Latin emittere "send forth," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + mittere "to send" (see mission).

meanings.definitionCn: 种类，部属，类目；范畴，类型

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French catégorie, from Late Latin categoria, from Greek kategoria "accusation, prediction, category," verbal noun from kategorein "to speak against; to accuse, assert, predicate," from kata "down to" (or perhaps "against;" see cata-) + agoreuein "to harangue, to declaim (in the assembly)," from agora "public assembly" (see agora).

meanings.definitionCn: 突然折断，拉断；猛咬；啪地关上
